Of course, no longer.Libraries are certainly declining in their traditional form. I find it odd that everyone has a digital resources in their pocket yet libraries are squeezing out physical books to make way for more and more computers. Try to find paper copy of the Sony founder's book.... that will be £50 on amazon. Prohibitive. No library within a 10 mile radius has a copy.I remember joyfully discovering Tony Royce's book in my library a few years ago. That enlightenment will never happen now. Primary knowledge is being lost, churned crude will forever lubricate the delusions of those who have no facility to collate the basis of our understanding. > The internet, in its early days felt like that. Of course, no longer.back in the reasonably early days of the web (I would guess ~2000) I stumbled across a webpage that said "I used to collect random interesting snippets in a shoebox, here is the internet version of it". I was delighted enough that I actually wrote to the author to compliment them on helping keep the web interesting, and though I haven't thought about it in ages it clearly made enough of an impression on me that I remembered the author's name. and it's still up! https://www-users.york.ac.uk/~ss44/cyc/index.htm AIs are fantastic for discovery. Just today I asked Chatgpt to find blogs 1) about old school newspaper cartoons 2) that have been writing for more than 10 years and 3) are run by one or two passionate people rather than a team. Within seconds Chatgpt found 7 candidates. Within another handful of seconds it gave me RSS links to the ones I wanted.This is incredible! Sometimes. I think this is partly because search engines are so poor nowadays. I have asked AI for certain websites that I know exist but it can't find them. I would really like this, but using academic reading lists instead of prizes. What are the important historians of (e.g.) medieval Italy? This is wonderful. 30 seconds into trying the site and I’ve already found a couple of interesting books to read. The search I typed is ‘Stuff being made, how it was made, how history shaped its form’. Is it even possible to find one these days? I would love to see some real honest amazon kindle stats on the amount of ebooks added over the last 3 years. Even a honest pie chart to show people its not worth it to get them to stop posting them. I like very very much that there's no user voting system here.I just recently read a really mediocre book with a 4.7/5 rating on Goodreads. Shows being sloppy is not exclusively a privilege for neither the AIs or producers. This is pretty nice, I might use this if I'm looking to learn more on a topic.I'm always cautious when reading nonfiction because it's hard for me to tell if the author knows what they're talking about when I'm not an expert myself. Using awards is a good metric. Maybe.I am curious what the "score" for each book means. Is it calculated by giving each award a certain weight and adding them up? Awesome stuff!Now, can this interface with Libby? The worldcat interface is, well, confusing. I can't figure out if it knows libraries near me exist. I'm not sure how I feel about "Entangled Life" on the first page of books in "science". Isn't that slop? I mean, it's pop-science bullshit, and it's based on feelgood extrapolation from a thin evidence base. Maybe that doesn't quite fit the definition of slop, but it's not a signal of quality for the rest of the list. The antithesis of AI slop is when you've created a unique idea from first principles and AI keeps misunderstanding your idea and spell checking it back to something that's widely popular such that you have to write a whole extra alignment subsystem to check that it hasn't ignored half of what you've wrote in your first principles document and just thought you were talking about something distantly related in its training set.To work on something truly original in non-fiction in the future against AI pushing everything to the mean of its training set and fighting against that when doing research, as opposed to generating a whole book of slop from familiar ideas, is going to be quite the unrecognized effort of writing those books. possibly a bug - I searched for "writers like lewis thomas" and the top two results were by thomas himself. I really thought ChatGPT would be able to tell me what this is called but it can't. I suggest "circular eponym".It's like calling a CRT monitor a really big flatscreen. For me it is surprisngly natural to include the original thing into "a set of thing-likes".Why does it feel wrong (or meaningless) to you? Is it only me or the titles of top books from History, Nature, and Politics sound very click-baiting? I'm really taken off by these titles. I participate book club for several years with friends, we casually navigate certain topics, and i can tell you human slop in literature is a real thing.- almost every book try to stretch core idea into book size format- unique ideas are rare people attack them under different angels- a later phenomena : their believes almost predict entire book, outcomes etc, brainwash impact is realso not sure, how ai slop is better vs book slop, at least with ai you can distill the idea, with the book, you have to spend 10-40 hours to digest average, absolutely non fresh ideas, that author brought in just to sell that book, otherwise it would be magazine article worth. In your experience, how do you determine book slop? For me I don't really stray from authors and topics I like, and non-fiction isn't really my go to outside of sci-fi, and mystery/law books. Like on the stretch point, I can tell that in other media but not so much books. Self improvement books! All of them! "Make good habits, not bad habits because this is what Lincoln did..." This is sadly correct/true. This is one of the many reasons why I militantly reject carbon chauvinism. Most of the potshots people take against AI can be easily retorted with “have you seen how bad the average human is at X?” > how ai slop is better vs book slop, at least with ai you can distill the ideaWhy don't you just use whatever LLM you have on hand to summarize every book you could read instead of reading it? The idea that ai slop is 'better' because you can distill the idea is an insane point to me because it treats books as a pure consumption-based concept, where the goal is strictly to finish a book and move on.I think even the worst book is more valuable than whatever garbage an LLM writes because you can at least learn from the intent. Where and why the author failed, where they got stuck, the points they failed to make. LLM writing is a void, there's nothing to be learned. Thank you! Wonderful idea, and very well executed. Useful discovery tool.I understand your passion is non-fiction, but would be wonderful to have the equivalent for fiction, of which there is a great deal of excellent literature. Perhaps the same site could incorporate both fiction and non-fiction, with a filter? I made a sandwich yesterday and cried for an hour because it reminded me of the way my mom used to make school lunches. No AI necessary. I don't think we would have AI slop, if they were just trained on high quality media.Less relatable AI, maybe, but not sloppy AI.AI trained on other AI (i.e. distilling), can be better than the AI they were trained on, likely because of this reason. Each iteration can raise the bar.As apposed to the deep spiral of generating shiny attention crystals for humans, and then training on that. The slop isn't a property of the LLM but the workflow it's utilized in. Just now I asked ChatGPT about the linguistic effect where a CRT could be described as a giant flatscreen, a landline could be described as a wired cellphone, or the original Rogue could be described as a roguelike. Unfortunately it doesn't know what that's called either, but that is a non-slop use of an LLM, like a semantic search engine. On the other hand if you print LLM output into a book it's almost certainly slop no matter how the model was trained. Training AI on AI will most likely increase slop. LLM's add randomness in their outputs, books usually don't. My bona-fides here amount to little more than being a big time reader of non-fiction. But IMO, there's a nice synthesis here. Rather than going long rounds of asking LLMs about a subject, I usually end up asking it for book recommendations. It's much better than a google search and you can push it into some deep corners if you go past the surface level recs.You can get quite specific. You can find texts you wouldn't discover unless you spent years studying the topic. Often these are completely approachable and give interesting perspectives they just get buried behind a wall of syllabi and listicles.This is how I ended up reading Thompson's 'The Making of the English Working Class' and Graves' 'Goodbye to all That' among others. I did this for a recent trip! I asked books related to or authored in the places I was visiting. Found some interesting titles. I read at night in bed. I love how, with ebooks (first Overdrive, now Libby), when I hear about an interesting book, I can within 30 seconds search for it across multiple libraries, borrow it, and send it to my Kindle. And what pray tell is everyone doing with their privileged leisure time?You have access to every single wicked problem in the world and you choose to do nothing.Shame It's sad now to see a university library where students sit among endless shelves of amazing books, sitting on laptops, almost all of them with ChatGPT open. The library has become just a place to sit and open an LLM. The most sad part of all is to think how little of that knowledge is digitized and available to be referenced by ChatGPT. Digitizing old books is one of the highest things you can do as a human for “good for the world”. It's especially sad because on of the things those LLMs are best, almost purpose built for is to tell those students which books to go open, where to find nuggets people haven't bumped into for years, to make cross-connections that would take a PhD a decade to find. It's a clever title because it's FREE MONEY on HN. There is a small but rabid group that upvotes everything that denigrates AI, sight unseen. Nice site, but most of the top biographies are US figures: Edgar Hoover, Frederick Douglass, Lyndon Johnson etc. I'd like to see more non-US biographies. Can you please review the site guidelines (https://news.ycombinator.com/newsguidelines.html) and stick to them when posting? You broke several of them here."Don't be snarky.""Omit internet tropes." (the stopped-reading-at bit)"Please don't post shallow dismissals, especially of other people's work. A good critical comment teaches us something."Edit: it looks like your account has been posting quite a few flamebait and/or unsubstantive comments generally. Could you please not do that? It's not what this site is for, and destroys what it is for. I think my issue with this project--and so many other similar ones--is that the provenance of the code does undermine the intention. If a project purports to be about quality, then knowing that the creator abdicated some of the responsibility for creating the thing they ostensibly care about makes it harder to put faith in them as having high standards elsewhere.Perhaps I am just old-fashioned, and vibe-coding is something that can be done with full focus and care for high quality, but I remain unconvinced. This is a project that needs to be cared about sincerely to be trustable/meaningful/useful, and the approach taken casts doubt on that.

blocks.html_en:
Not to be intentionally contrarian, but I have thought a lot about this:

I think good fiction is actually the antithesis of AI, fundamentally. LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into output. This can give you some creative results, but it is ultimately kind of limited. I cannot imagine that prompting an AI a thousand times would give you something truly original in the way many high-quality fictional stories are. The magic and absurdity of life is a necessary thing to create such works.

sentences.en: Not to be intentionally contrarian, but I have thought a lot about this:I think good fiction is actually the antithesis of AI, fundamentally.
sentences.zh: 并不是故意逆反，但我对此思考了很多：我认为从根本上来说，好的小说实际上是人工智能的对立面。

sentences.en: LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into output.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士实际上只能将不同的东西组合成输出。

sentences.en: This can give you some creative results, but it is ultimately kind of limited.
sentences.zh: 这可以给你一些创造性的结果，但它最终是有限的。

sentences.en: I cannot imagine that prompting an AI a thousand times would give you something truly original in the way many high-quality fictional stories are.
sentences.zh: 我无法想象提示人工智能一千次会给你带来像许多高质量虚构故事那样真正原创的东西。

sentences.en: The magic and absurdity of life is a necessary thing to create such works.
sentences.zh: 生活的魔幻与荒诞正是创作此类作品所必需的东西。

blocks.html_en:
> LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into output

> The magic and absurdity of life is a necessary thing to create such works.

These two points seem slightly conflicting. Why if a human is truly more creative than AI, must it experience "the magic and absurdity of life" if it can be creative without "combining things into output"? Is a creative human then just not splicing and recombining their experiences into their creative output? And therefore, if an AI had more data that mirrors that experience in some way would it be sufficiently creative to create something "truly original"?

My view is that intelligence and creativity is all just purely recombination. AI can do that well just as humans can. The gap is that the data humans have access to is much more analogue, emotional, and grounded in the real world compared to the data that AI is currently trained on.

sentences.en: > LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into output> The magic and absurdity of life is a necessary thing to create such works.These two points seem slightly conflicting.
sentences.zh: > LLM确实只能将不同的东西组合成输出> 生活的神奇和荒诞是创作这样的作品所必需的。这两点似乎有点矛盾。

sentences.en: Why if a human is truly more creative than AI, must it experience "the magic and absurdity of life" if it can be creative without "combining things into output"?
sentences.zh: 为什么如果人类确实比人工智能更具创造力，如果人类不需要“将事物组合成输出”就能发挥创造力，那么它必须体验“生命的神奇和荒谬”吗？

sentences.en: Is a creative human then just not splicing and recombining their experiences into their creative output?
sentences.zh: 难道一个有创造力的人不会将他们的经验拼接并重新组合成他们的创造性产出吗？

sentences.en: And therefore, if an AI had more data that mirrors that experience in some way would it be sufficiently creative to create something "truly original"?My view is that intelligence and creativity is all just purely recombination.
sentences.zh: 因此，如果人工智能拥有更多以某种方式反映这种体验的数据，它是否具有足够的创造力来创造“真正原创”的东西？我的观点是，智力和创造力都只是纯粹的重组。

sentences.en: AI can do that well just as humans can.
sentences.zh: 人工智能可以像人类一样做得很好。

sentences.zh: 差距在于，与人工智能目前训练的数据相比，人类可以获得的数据更加模拟、更加情绪化、更加扎根于现实世界。

> And therefore, if an AI had more data that mirrors that experience in some way would it be sufficiently creative to create something "truly original"?

An important distinction seems to lie in a human having original experiences to draw from, while an AI has only "data that mirrors" experiences.

blocks.html_en:
That's what we all did in school, didn't we?

Nobody did any original research, you didn't discover gravity, and you didn't participate in any historically significant events.

Yet we say that people who have been through education know about such things.

blocks.html_en:
That is descriptive knowledge. Not firsthand qualia.

You may know every detail of the American Civil War, from every book ever written on the topic. You may read every journal from every person that wrote one about it.

You do not have firsthand knowledge of what it was like to participate in the war. Any assumption of that knowledge is a (false) generalization of what you think a person “would have thought or felt” based on contemporary ideas of the personality, psychology, etc. Reading a journal entry about a soldier seeing his comrade killed is not equivalent to actually having that experience yourself.

blocks.html_en:
That’s a pretty lacking theory of knowledge. How can you know what taste is, if you’re never tasted anything?

If you read a thousand books about living in New York City, is that the same thing as the experiential knowledge that comes from living there in person? I certainly don’t think so.

sentences.zh: 这是一种非常缺乏的知识理论。

sentences.en: I certainly don’t think so.
sentences.zh: 我当然不这么认为。

blocks.html_en:
> while an AI has only "data that mirrors" experiences.

True for a lot of current AI. With more robotic bodies, loads more access to cameras/audio/satellite/science measurements, an AI can have original experience on a level that can only be described as god-like compared to humans.

blocks.html_en:
You could argue AI already partly has access to these measurements. You could easily tap into smart sensor data etc, same voor video/audio etc.

The part that AI can't (yet) truly replicate is 'feelings/emotions'. Experiences such feeling dread, loneliness, happiness. Or being anxious, nervous or having goosebumps. You can describe all these states but they are differently experienced for each individual and also dependent on each unique situation.

Ofcourse this also then comes down to how you exactly define emotions.

blocks.html_en:
And at this point you’re far off into science fiction, and beyond reasonable discussion of this.

Even then, it’s not really clear to me that an android with the exact appearance of a person will have the same experiences. They aren’t biological creatures with millions of years of evolution behind them.

blocks.html_en:
I think you're just neglecting the fact that LLMs only exist in the virtual world, whereas we're interacting with a world that's richer and has much more to offer. The virtual world is still just a mere subset of our world, but whether we want to perceive it as such is up to each individual. It certainly feels like you're conflating digital with analog, and a digital signal can still only approximate an analog one.

I still doubt that we have the knowledge to describe all of our human desires as mere results of our brain interacting with the nervous system either. There are many processes going on besides analysis and synthesis. If I plan on doing something extraordinary, I don't only sit there, think, and try to rearrange some pieces from memory. I'll go look around, touch, taste, re-explore and expand the perception my environment has to offer. Further, my perception can vary by the minute and I could even try to bring in a touch of chance whenever I feel like.

Wittgenstein's "whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent" might be fitting here, yet language still continues to evolve.

sentences.en: I think you're just neglecting the fact that LLMs only exist in the virtual world, whereas we're interacting with a world that's richer and has much more to offer.
sentences.zh: 我认为你只是忽略了一个事实，即法学硕士只存在于虚拟世界中，而我们正在与一个更丰富、有更多东西可以提供的世界互动。

sentences.en: The virtual world is still just a mere subset of our world, but whether we want to perceive it as such is up to each individual.
sentences.zh: 虚拟世界仍然只是我们世界的一个子集，但我们是否想要这样感知它取决于每个人。

sentences.en: It certainly feels like you're conflating digital with analog, and a digital signal can still only approximate an analog one.I still doubt that we have the knowledge to describe all of our human desires as mere results of our brain interacting with the nervous system either.
sentences.zh: 当然感觉就像你将数字与模拟混为一谈，而数字信号仍然只能近似模拟信号。我仍然怀疑我们是否有知识将我们所有的人类欲望描述为我们的大脑与神经系统相互作用的结果。

sentences.en: There are many processes going on besides analysis and synthesis.
sentences.zh: 除了分析和综合之外，还有许多过程正在进行。

sentences.en: If I plan on doing something extraordinary, I don't only sit there, think, and try to rearrange some pieces from memory.
sentences.zh: 如果我打算做一些非凡的事情，我不会只是坐在那里思考，并尝试根据记忆重新排列一些片段。

sentences.en: I'll go look around, touch, taste, re-explore and expand the perception my environment has to offer.
sentences.zh: 我会环顾四周、触摸、品尝、重新探索和扩展我的环境所提供的感知。

sentences.en: Further, my perception can vary by the minute and I could even try to bring in a touch of chance whenever I feel like.Wittgenstein's "whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent" might be fitting here, yet language still continues to evolve.
sentences.zh: 此外，我的感知可能每时每刻都在变化，我什至可以在任何愿意的时候尝试引入一点机会。维特根斯坦的“一个人不能说话的地方，一个人必须保持沉默”在这里可能很合适，但语言仍然在不断发展。

blocks.html_en:
I think you're just neglecting the fact that LLMs only exist in the virtual world, whereas we're interacting with a world that's richer and has much more to offer. The virtual world is still just a mere subset of our world, but whether we want to perceive it as such is up to each individual. It certainly feels like you're conflating digital with analog, and a digital signal can still only approximate an analog one.

I still doubt that we have the knowledge to describe all of our human desires as mere results of our brain interacting with the nervous system either. There are many processes going on besides analysis and synthesis. If I plan on doing something extraordinary, I don't only sit there, think, and try to rearrange some pieces from memory. I'll go look around, touch, taste, re-explore and expand the perception my environment has to offer. Further, my perception can vary by the minute and I could even try to bring in a touch of chance whenever I feel like.

Wittgenstein's "whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent" might be fitting here, yet language still continues to evolve.

blocks.html_en:
Mathematical proofs are hard to come to, but easy (or “cheap”) to verify. Also having a math proof processed through a transformer to derive an output that matches existing proof patterns kid of makes sense. The former allows you to generate hundreds or thousands of iteration and cheaply verifying its correctness until you hit the right randomness level. Generating thousands of fiction books then verify them doesn’t really work.

Most good fiction authors are trying to communicate a story that’s forming in their minds. Even when they get stuck, they are usually not stuck because they just don’t know anyone to ask. It would’t be their voice anymore. Some do seek input and those might experiment with llms for ideas. Using llms for research, technical questions, grammar, synonym sentences, word selection, etc makes sense too.

I’d agree with OP for people generating full stories through llms. While I say that, I know a very young person who told a while back (like early ChatGPT 4 time) they use it to write fan fiction for books or characters they like.

sentences.en: Mathematical proofs are hard to come to, but easy (or “cheap”) to verify.
sentences.zh: 数学证明很难获得，但验证起来很容易（或“便宜”）。

sentences.en: Also having a math proof processed through a transformer to derive an output that matches existing proof patterns kid of makes sense.
sentences.zh: 此外，通过变压器处理数学证明以得出与现有证明模式相匹配的输出是有意义的。

sentences.en: The former allows you to generate hundreds or thousands of iteration and cheaply verifying its correctness until you hit the right randomness level.
sentences.zh: 前者允许您生成数百或数千次迭代，并以低廉的成本验证其正确性，直到达到正确的随机性水平。

sentences.zh: 生成数千本小说，然后验证它们是否真的有效。大多数优秀的小说作者都在尝试传达他们脑海中正在形成的故事。

blocks.html_en:
> LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into output

In terms of how writers think about creativity this doesn’t reflect reality- there’s the old writer’s saying that there are only 7 stories (and other variations of this idea) and that writing is about creatively remixing these well-worn ideas.

The LLM’s capability to write acceptable fiction and nonfiction is coming soon if it’s not already here. (I think right now it still needs some high level input from humans, depending on length and topic)

It’s clear to me that, especially as LLMs get better and better, there’s only one real difference- it’s that I don’t want to hear what an LLM has to say. I’m only interested in what other humans have to say. I feel the same way about AI music- even if it sounds ok, even as good as the kind of unoriginal pop music that’s not AI made (but is a kind of it’s own slop) or a bad committee made hollywood movie, it’s still fundamentally more interesting than something that’s been generated. Even if it’s 100% fiction it’s still based on a real human’s life experiences.

sentences.en: > LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into outputIn terms of how writers think about creativity this doesn’t reflect reality- there’s the old writer’s saying that there are only 7 stories (and other variations of this idea) and that writing is about creatively remixing these well-worn ideas.The LLM’s capability to write acceptable fiction and nonfiction is coming soon if it’s not already here.
sentences.zh: > 法学硕士实际上只能将不同的东西组合成输出就作家如何看待创造力而言，这并不能反映现实——老作家说只有7个故事（以及这个想法的其他变体），而写作就是创造性地重新混合这些陈旧的想法。法学硕士写出可接受的小说和非小说的能力很快就会出现，如果它还没有出现的话。

sentences.en: (I think right now it still needs some high level input from humans, depending on length and topic)It’s clear to me that, especially as LLMs get better and better, there’s only one real difference- it’s that I don’t want to hear what an LLM has to say.
sentences.zh: （我认为现在它仍然需要人类的一些高水平的输入，具体取决于长度和主题）我很清楚，特别是随着法学硕士变得越来越好，只有一个真正的区别——那就是我不想听法学硕士必须说的话。

sentences.en: I’m only interested in what other humans have to say.
sentences.zh: 我只对其他人说的话感兴趣。

sentences.en: I feel the same way about AI music- even if it sounds ok, even as good as the kind of unoriginal pop music that’s not AI made (but is a kind of it’s own slop) or a bad committee made hollywood movie, it’s still fundamentally more interesting than something that’s been generated.
sentences.zh: 我对人工智能音乐也有同样的感觉——即使它听起来不错，甚至和那种不是人工智能制作的非原创流行音乐（而是一种它自己的废话）或一部糟糕的委员会制作的好莱坞电影一样好，它从根本上仍然比生成的东西更有趣。

sentences.en: Even if it’s 100% fiction it’s still based on a real human’s life experiences.
sentences.zh: 即使它100%是虚构的，它仍然是基于真实人类的生活经历。

blocks.html_en:
> LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into output

In terms of how writers think about creativity this doesn’t reflect reality- there’s the old writer’s saying that there are only 7 stories (and other variations of this idea) and that writing is about creatively remixing these well-worn ideas.

The LLM’s capability to write acceptable fiction and nonfiction is coming soon if it’s not already here. (I think right now it still needs some high level input from humans, depending on length and topic)

It’s clear to me that, especially as LLMs get better and better, there’s only one real difference- it’s that I don’t want to hear what an LLM has to say. I’m only interested in what other humans have to say. I feel the same way about AI music- even if it sounds ok, even as good as the kind of unoriginal pop music that’s not AI made (but is a kind of it’s own slop) or a bad committee made hollywood movie, it’s still fundamentally more interesting than something that’s been generated. Even if it’s 100% fiction it’s still based on a real human’s life experiences.

sentences.en: > LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into outputIn terms of how writers think about creativity this doesn’t reflect reality- there’s the old writer’s saying that there are only 7 stories (and other variations of this idea) and that writing is about creatively remixing these well-worn ideas.The LLM’s capability to write acceptable fiction and nonfiction is coming soon if it’s not already here.
sentences.zh: > 法学硕士实际上只能将不同的东西组合成输出就作家如何看待创造力而言，这并不能反映现实——老作家说只有7个故事（以及这个想法的其他变体），而写作就是创造性地重新混合这些陈旧的想法。法学硕士写出可接受的小说和非小说的能力很快就会出现，如果它还没有出现的话。

sentences.en: (I think right now it still needs some high level input from humans, depending on length and topic)It’s clear to me that, especially as LLMs get better and better, there’s only one real difference- it’s that I don’t want to hear what an LLM has to say.
sentences.zh: （我认为现在它仍然需要人类的一些高水平的输入，具体取决于长度和主题）我很清楚，特别是随着法学硕士变得越来越好，只有一个真正的区别——那就是我不想听法学硕士必须说的话。

blocks.html_en:
This is a bit like saying “there are only five fundamental flavors” and everything is some combination of them.

Maybe, but that has basically zero effect on the experience of actually eating food. Just because the form may be the same doesn’t mean an LLM will be able to supply original content.

blocks.html_en:
I’m not sure I understand the analogy. You could say that the reason why artificial flavors work in food is because we know how to recreate flavors from first principle chemistry. The dislike of the artificialness of cheetos also doesn’t stop many many people from eating them.

I would say human made slop writing isn’t any better or worse quality than LLM writing.

Knowing if it was made by a real person makes a difference to me, but I also know it won’t to everyone.

sentences.en: I’m not sure I understand the analogy.
sentences.zh: 我不确定我是否理解这个类比。

sentences.en: You could say that the reason why artificial flavors work in food is because we know how to recreate flavors from first principle chemistry.
sentences.zh: 您可以说，人工香料之所以在食品中发挥作用，是因为我们知道如何从第一原理化学中重新创造风味。

sentences.en: The dislike of the artificialness of cheetos also doesn’t stop many many people from eating them.I would say human made slop writing isn’t any better or worse quality than LLM writing.Knowing if it was made by a real person makes a difference to me, but I also know it won’t to everyone.
sentences.zh: 对人造奇多的厌恶也并不能阻止很多人吃它们。我想说，人类制作的草稿写作并不比法学硕士写作的质量更好或更差。知道它是否是由真人制作的对我来说很重要，但我也知道这对每个人来说都不会。

blocks.html_en:
Just because the form of a story is the same as another story does not imply that the content is interchangeable.

I could write you a story right now about being an expat living in Poland in 2026. It’s unclear to me how an LLM would have the cultural knowledge or experience to write such a story that is actually accurate and up to date, without already having something like my story already in its dataset.

sentences.en: Just because the form of a story is the same as another story does not imply that the content is interchangeable.I could write you a story right now about being an expat living in Poland in 2026.
sentences.zh: 仅仅因为一个故事的形式与另一个故事相同，并不意味着内容可以互换。我现在可以给你写一个关于 2026 年住在波兰的外籍人士的故事。

sentences.en: It’s unclear to me how an LLM would have the cultural knowledge or experience to write such a story that is actually accurate and up to date, without already having something like my story already in its dataset.
sentences.zh: 我不清楚法学硕士如何拥有文化知识或经验来写这样一个实际上准确且最新的故事，而数据集中却没有像我的故事这样的东西。

blocks.html_en:
Which would you rather watch:

Superhero Wars Trek 42 - the latest franchise installment, billion dollar Hollywood budget, financed and produced by billionaires, audience tested committee-made script, lots of people involved, designed primarily to push merchandise.

Personal Story - a movie about someone's personal reality or fantasy, something typically not made by studios because the story isn't profitable to a wide audience, painstakingly generated and edited by one person using AI, designed primarily to tell a story.

I put up a different strawman because your strawman is too beatable. As humans, of course we're all interested in what other humans have to say. But you haven't explained why you feel people can't use AI to say things, or why AI output can't be based on a real human's life experiences.\

Personally, it seems like movie studios are the ones with only 7 stories to tell, and I've seen the movie and its sequel, the prequel, the requel, the remake, the gritty reboot, the musical, and the animated series. I'm excited that AI will let people skip the studio gatekeepers to tell some new stories for a change.

sentences.en: Which would you rather watch:Superhero Wars Trek 42 - the latest franchise installment, billion dollar Hollywood budget, financed and produced by billionaires, audience tested committee-made script, lots of people involved, designed primarily to push merchandise.Personal Story - a movie about someone's personal reality or fantasy, something typically not made by studios because the story isn't profitable to a wide audience, painstakingly generated and edited by one person using AI, designed primarily to tell a story.I put up a different strawman because your strawman is too beatable.
sentences.zh: 你更喜欢看哪一个：超级英雄战争迷航 42 - 最新的系列电影，数十亿美元的好莱坞预算，由亿万富翁资助和制作，经过观众测试的委员会制作的剧本，很多人参与，主要旨在推销商品。个人故事 - 一部关于某人的个人现实或幻想的电影，通常不是由工作室制作的，因为这个故事对广大观众来说没有利润，由一个人使用人工智能精心生成和编辑，主要设计用于讲述一个故事。我提出了另一个稻草人因为你的稻草人太难打了。

sentences.en: As humans, of course we're all interested in what other humans have to say.
sentences.zh: 作为人类，我们当然都对其他人所说的话感兴趣。

sentences.en: But you haven't explained why you feel people can't use AI to say things, or why AI output can't be based on a real human's life experiences.\Personally, it seems like movie studios are the ones with only 7 stories to tell, and I've seen the movie and its sequel, the prequel, the requel, the remake, the gritty reboot, the musical, and the animated series.
sentences.zh: 但你还没有解释为什么你觉得人们不能用人工智能来说话，或者为什么人工智能输出不能基于真实人类的生活经历。\就我个人而言，电影制片厂似乎只有7个故事可讲，我看过这部电影及其续集、前传、后传、翻拍、坚韧重启、音乐剧和动画系列。

sentences.en: I'm excited that AI will let people skip the studio gatekeepers to tell some new stories for a change.
sentences.zh: 我很高兴人工智能将让人们跳过工作室的看门人来讲述一些新的故事以进行改变。

sentences.zh: 个人故事 - 一部关于某人的个人现实或幻想的电影，通常不是由工作室制作的，因为这个故事对广大观众来说没有利润，由一个人使用人工智能精心制作和编辑，主要旨在讲述一个故事。

blocks.html_en: I put up a different strawman because your strawman is too beatable. As humans, of course we're all interested in what other humans have to say. But you haven't explained why you feel people can't use AI to say things, or why AI output can't be based on a real human's life experiences.\

sentences.en: I put up a different strawman because your strawman is too beatable.
sentences.zh: 我放了一个不同的稻草人，因为你的稻草人太容易被打败了。

sentences.en: As humans, of course we're all interested in what other humans have to say.
sentences.zh: 作为人类，我们当然都对其他人所说的话感兴趣。

sentences.en: But you haven't explained why you feel people can't use AI to say things, or why AI output can't be based on a real human's life experiences.\
sentences.zh: 但你没有解释为什么你觉得人们不能用人工智能说话，或者为什么人工智能的输出不能基于真实人类的生活经历。\

blocks.html_en: Personally, it seems like movie studios are the ones with only 7 stories to tell, and I've seen the movie and its sequel, the prequel, the requel, the remake, the gritty reboot, the musical, and the animated series. I'm excited that AI will let people skip the studio gatekeepers to tell some new stories for a change.

sentences.en: Personally, it seems like movie studios are the ones with only 7 stories to tell, and I've seen the movie and its sequel, the prequel, the requel, the remake, the gritty reboot, the musical, and the animated series.
sentences.zh: 就我个人而言，电影制片厂似乎只有 7 个故事可讲，我看过这部电影及其续集、前传、后传、翻拍版、坚韧不拔的重启版、音乐剧和动画系列。

sentences.en: I'm excited that AI will let people skip the studio gatekeepers to tell some new stories for a change.
sentences.zh: 我很高兴人工智能将让人们跳过工作室的看门人来讲述一些新的故事以进行改变。

blocks.html_en:
So now we'll have to investigate what "original" means?

From what I can see, AI is absolutely able to create new work. Things that are not in the dataset, but look like they could be. This is pretty much what generalising means, they can make a wide variety of things that aren't actually in the original set but can hide in that set pretty well.

When you are an original piece of culture, what do you mean by it?

sentences.en: So now we'll have to investigate what "original" means?From what I can see, AI is absolutely able to create new work.
sentences.zh: 那么现在我们要研究一下什么是“原创”？以我看来，AI绝对能够创造出新的作品。

sentences.en: Things that are not in the dataset, but look like they could be.
sentences.zh: 数据集中不存在但看起来可能存在的事物。

sentences.en: This is pretty much what generalising means, they can make a wide variety of things that aren't actually in the original set but can hide in that set pretty well.When you are an original piece of culture, what do you mean by it?
sentences.zh: 这几乎就是泛化的意思，他们可以创造出各种各样的东西，这些东西实际上不在原始集合中，但可以很好地隐藏在那个集合中。当你是一个原始文化时，你的意思是什么？

blocks.html_en:
Can an AI write a story about living as an artist in NYC in 2026, the cultural environment, etc. without that information being added to the dataset immediately as it happens?

It seems pretty obvious to me that there are cultural trends which are happening instantaneously and aren’t merely some sort of consequence of past historical data.

sentences.en: Can an AI write a story about living as an artist in NYC in 2026, the cultural environment, etc. without that information being added to the dataset immediately as it happens?It seems pretty obvious to me that there are cultural trends which are happening instantaneously and aren’t merely some sort of consequence of past historical data.
sentences.zh: 人工智能是否可以写一个关于 2026 年作为一名艺术家在纽约生活、文化环境等的故事，而无需在发生时立即将这些信息添加到数据集中？对我来说，很明显，有些文化趋势是瞬时发生的，而不仅仅是过去历史数据的某种结果。

LLMs are really only capable of combining different things into output

what makes believe humans can do more than this ?

it's a real question. I've seen a lot of people stating that the human mind "is more than this" as if it was obvious

sentences.zh: 法学硕士实际上只能将不同的事物组合成输出，是什么让人们相信人类可以做的比这更多？这是一个真正的问题。

sentences.zh: 我见过很多人说人类的思想“不止于此”，好像这是显而易见的

blocks.html_en:
I would say the inherently chaotic and continuous nature of the human brain can create novelty in a fundamentally different way than LLMs can.

Like a human can reach into the chaos of their own emotions and pull something unique out. There is some layer of the human experience where ideas can ferment and change based on all your memories is weird ways, and LLMs completely lack this, which makes things LLMs create very blunt and obvious. Like there is no nuance to anything they do.

sentences.en: I would say the inherently chaotic and continuous nature of the human brain can create novelty in a fundamentally different way than LLMs can.Like a human can reach into the chaos of their own emotions and pull something unique out.
sentences.zh: 我想说，人脑固有的混乱和连续性可以以一种与法学硕士完全不同的方式创造新奇事物。就像人类可以深入到自己情感的混乱中，并从中提取出独特的东西一样。

sentences.en: There is some layer of the human experience where ideas can ferment and change based on all your memories is weird ways, and LLMs completely lack this, which makes things LLMs create very blunt and obvious.
sentences.zh: 在人类经验的某些层面上，想法可以根据你所有的记忆发酵和改变，这是很奇怪的方式，而法学硕士完全缺乏这一点，这使得法学硕士创造的东西非常生硬和明显。

sentences.en: Like there is no nuance to anything they do.
sentences.zh: 就像他们所做的任何事情都没有细微差别一样。

Historians who are not trying to sell mass market books have a much broader set of books. You won't see them in a normal bookstore.

blocks.html_en: I would bet that how your brain stores information that you read from long-form text is very different from how it stores information you acquire from chatting with an LLM. When I read something challenging or new to me I spend a lot of time thinking about how what I'm reading matches my own experiences or knowledge. Although I'm a fairly fast reader, it often takes me a long time to get through difficult pages since I have to stop and think about what I'm reading. I seem to be doing a lot of integrating and reorganizing my thoughts. When interacting with LLMs it feels a lot more like I'm just receiving knowledge passively and I don't think it gets integrated as well. Not sure why this is and its somewhat counterintuitive since I don't think I'd have the same experience with a human tutor.

sentences.en: I would bet that how your brain stores information that you read from long-form text is very different from how it stores information you acquire from chatting with an LLM.
sentences.zh: 我敢打赌，你的大脑存储从长文本中读取的信息的方式与存储从与法学硕士聊天中获得的信息的方式非常不同。

sentences.en: When I read something challenging or new to me I spend a lot of time thinking about how what I'm reading matches my own experiences or knowledge.
sentences.zh: 当我读到一些对我来说具有挑战性或新鲜的东西时，我会花很多时间思考我所读的内容如何与我自己的经验或知识相匹配。

sentences.en: Although I'm a fairly fast reader, it often takes me a long time to get through difficult pages since I have to stop and think about what I'm reading.
sentences.zh: 虽然我的阅读速度相当快，但我经常要花很长时间才能读完困难的页面，因为我必须停下来思考我正在阅读的内容。

sentences.en: I seem to be doing a lot of integrating and reorganizing my thoughts.
sentences.zh: 我似乎正在做很多整合和重组我的想法。

sentences.en: When interacting with LLMs it feels a lot more like I'm just receiving knowledge passively and I don't think it gets integrated as well.
sentences.zh: 当与法学硕士互动时，感觉更像是我只是被动地接收知识，而且我认为它也没有得到整合。

sentences.en: Not sure why this is and its somewhat counterintuitive since I don't think I'd have the same experience with a human tutor.
sentences.zh: 不知道为什么会这样，而且有点违反直觉，因为我认为我不会与人类导师有同样的经历。

blocks.html_en: I would bet that how your brain stores information that you read from long-form text is very different from how it stores information you acquire from chatting with an LLM. When I read something challenging or new to me I spend a lot of time thinking about how what I'm reading matches my own experiences or knowledge. Although I'm a fairly fast reader, it often takes me a long time to get through difficult pages since I have to stop and think about what I'm reading. I seem to be doing a lot of integrating and reorganizing my thoughts. When interacting with LLMs it feels a lot more like I'm just receiving knowledge passively and I don't think it gets integrated as well. Not sure why this is and its somewhat counterintuitive since I don't think I'd have the same experience with a human tutor.

sentences.en: I would bet that how your brain stores information that you read from long-form text is very different from how it stores information you acquire from chatting with an LLM.
sentences.zh: 我敢打赌，你的大脑存储从长文本中读取的信息的方式与存储从与法学硕士聊天中获得的信息的方式非常不同。

blocks.html_en:
What you're describing is very close to what education theory calls "assimilation" and "accommodation". When we assimilate knowledge, it just fits into our schema of understanding. When we accommodate knowledge, we need to change our schema, and that is where the feeling of being challenged (and often the feeling of profundity) comes from.

For example, a child learns that "foreigner" means someone from outside their country. Then, when they're 11, they go on their first holiday abroad and realise "Wait! _I_ am a foreigner here!"

So, maybe one way to frame what you're saying, is that LLM output tends towards being easily assimilable.

sentences.en: What you're describing is very close to what education theory calls "assimilation" and "accommodation".
sentences.zh: 你所描述的与教育理论所说的“同化”和“适应”非常接近。

sentences.en: When we assimilate knowledge, it just fits into our schema of understanding.
sentences.zh: 当我们吸收知识时，它恰好符合我们的理解模式。

sentences.en: When we accommodate knowledge, we need to change our schema, and that is where the feeling of being challenged (and often the feeling of profundity) comes from.For example, a child learns that "foreigner" means someone from outside their country.
sentences.zh: 当我们容纳知识时，我们需要改变我们的图式，这就是受到挑战的感觉（通常是深刻的感觉）的来源。例如，一个孩子了解到“外国人”意味着来自国外的人。

sentences.en: Then, when they're 11, they go on their first holiday abroad and realise "Wait! _I_ am a foreigner here!"So, maybe one way to frame what you're saying, is that LLM output tends towards being easily assimilable.
sentences.zh: 然后，当他们 11 岁时，他们第一次出国度假，并意识到“等等！_我_在这里是外国人！”因此，也许可以解释您所说的话的一种方式是，LLM 输出往往很容易被同化。

blocks.html_en:
I listen to audiobooks while I walk and hike, and sometimes when I recall some particular thing I've learned, I can also recall where I was hiking when I heard it.

I think there's a lot to learn on how we really process information.

sentences.en: I listen to audiobooks while I walk and hike, and sometimes when I recall some particular thing I've learned, I can also recall where I was hiking when I heard it.I think there's a lot to learn on how we really process information.
sentences.zh: 我在散步和徒步旅行时听有声读物，有时当我回忆起我学到的一些特定的东西时，我也能回忆起当我听到它时我正在徒步旅行的地方。我认为关于我们如何真正处理信息，还有很多东西需要学习。

blocks.html_en:
I once heard a lecture about „learning how to learn“. There are already a lot of well-researched things one can exploit about the way we process information. What you describe is known as retrieval cue. Our minds seem to be associative^1. It also seems a lot of the learning/processing happens after the actual learning process, which is why resting is important. Spaced repitition is a further method well-proven in practice.

^1 In the early days of AI, the concept of Hebbian learning was popular („neurons that fire together, wire together“). Its implementation is even simpler than gradient descent used today, but never caught on https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hebbian_theory

sentences.en: I once heard a lecture about „learning how to learn“.
sentences.zh: 我曾经听过一场关于“学习如何学习”的讲座。

sentences.en: There are already a lot of well-researched things one can exploit about the way we process information.
sentences.zh: 关于我们处理信息的方式，已经有很多经过深入研究的东西可以利用。

sentences.en: What you describe is known as retrieval cue.
sentences.zh: 你所描述的被称为检索线索。

sentences.en: Our minds seem to be associative^1.
sentences.zh: 我们的思维似乎具有联想能力^1。

sentences.en: It also seems a lot of the learning/processing happens after the actual learning process, which is why resting is important.
sentences.zh: 似乎很多学习/处理都是在实际学习过程之后发生的，这就是为什么休息很重要。

sentences.en: Spaced repitition is a further method well-proven in practice.^1 In the early days of AI, the concept of Hebbian learning was popular („neurons that fire together, wire together“).
sentences.zh: 间隔重复是一种在实践中得到充分证明的进一步方法。^1 在人工智能的早期，赫布学习的概念很流行（“神经元一起放电，连接在一起”）。

sentences.en: Its implementation is even simpler than gradient descent used today, but never caught on https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hebbian_theory
sentences.zh: 它的实现甚至比今天使用的梯度下降更简单，但从未在 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hebbian_theory 上流行起来

blocks.html_en:
That's spatial memory! I have long walk-and-talks with friends, and sometimes I forget minor details of previous discussions, but not where we were when it was mentioned.

A lot of my friends can't do same trick. But the fascinating part is that it still works for them! Usually they can recall whatever I forgotten after I described the place we were in.

sentences.en: That's spatial memory!
sentences.zh: 这就是空间记忆！

sentences.en: I have long walk-and-talks with friends, and sometimes I forget minor details of previous discussions, but not where we were when it was mentioned.A lot of my friends can't do same trick.
sentences.zh: 我和朋友们散步聊天时间很长，有时我会忘记之前讨论的一些小细节，但不记得我们提到这件事时我们所处的位置。我的很多朋友都做不到同样的技巧。

sentences.en: But the fascinating part is that it still works for them!
sentences.zh: 但令人着迷的是，它仍然对他们有效！

sentences.en: Usually they can recall whatever I forgotten after I described the place we were in.
sentences.zh: 通常，在我描述了我们所在的地方后，他们可以回忆起我忘记的任何事情。

blocks.html_en: There's a podcast I listened to as it came out when I was a teenager. I'm now relistening to it over a decade later, and I distinctly remember walking home from the bus stop with it coming through wired earbuds from my ipod.

blocks.html_en: In college I used mathematica a lot while taking linear algebra. I ended up having to relearn a lot of that math later since I never really understood it at a deep level, although I was able to use it and apply it under class conditions. I feel like a lot of my LLM derived knowledge is similarly superficial.

sentences.zh: 在大学里，我在学习线性代数时经常使用mathematica。

sentences.en: I ended up having to relearn a lot of that math later since I never really understood it at a deep level, although I was able to use it and apply it under class conditions.

sentences.en: I feel like a lot of my LLM derived knowledge is similarly superficial.
sentences.zh: 我觉得我的很多 LLM 衍生知识同样很肤浅。

blocks.html_en: That's absolutely right. Math is difficult to understand without writing it down on paper. As one of my professors said, "something magical happens when you write down a problem from scratch and work it out". LLMs make people believe they are improving their productivity and that may be so, but it also removes that magic and removes that deep learning.

sentences.en: That's absolutely right.
sentences.zh: 这是绝对正确的。

sentences.en: Math is difficult to understand without writing it down on paper.
sentences.zh: 数学如果不写在纸上就很难理解。

sentences.en: As one of my professors said, "something magical happens when you write down a problem from scratch and work it out".
sentences.zh: 正如我的一位教授所说，“当你从头开始写下一个问题并解决它时，神奇的事情就会发生”。

sentences.en: LLMs make people believe they are improving their productivity and that may be so, but it also removes that magic and removes that deep learning.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士让人们相信他们正在提高生产力，事实可能如此，但它也消除了这种魔力并消除了深度学习。

blocks.html_en:
> When interacting with LLMs it feels a lot more like I'm just receiving knowledge passively and I don't think it gets integrated as well.

This sounds like it's more down to how the individual uses the tool. I am not someone who has even been particularly good at reading > learning the thing. I've only ever been capable of learning by doing and with capability to interrogate on the points I don't get. LLMs allow that in a way that is just not feasible with any human being whose tolerance of me would diminish rapidly.

In most cases, I will be writing down my understanding as I would in isolation from a primary source, building flash cards and actively practicing what I have learned, only with more capability to interrogate on the points I have difficulty understanding or need clarity on. Effectively, I am doing the following in a capacity I personally never had via any other means:

> I seem to be doing a lot of integrating and reorganizing my thoughts.

If you are using it as a slot machine of knowledge, and going from receiving > doing with no intermediate step I see how outcomes could differ.

sentences.en: > When interacting with LLMs it feels a lot more like I'm just receiving knowledge passively and I don't think it gets integrated as well.This sounds like it's more down to how the individual uses the tool.
sentences.zh: > 当与法学硕士互动时，感觉更像是我只是被动地接收知识，而且我认为它也没有得到整合。这听起来更取决于个人如何使用该工具。

sentences.en: I am not someone who has even been particularly good at reading > learning the thing.
sentences.zh: 我不是一个特别擅长阅读>学习的人。

sentences.en: I've only ever been capable of learning by doing and with capability to interrogate on the points I don't get.
sentences.zh: 我只能通过实践来学习，并有能力质疑我不明白的观点。

sentences.en: LLMs allow that in a way that is just not feasible with any human being whose tolerance of me would diminish rapidly.In most cases, I will be writing down my understanding as I would in isolation from a primary source, building flash cards and actively practicing what I have learned, only with more capability to interrogate on the points I have difficulty understanding or need clarity on.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士以一种对任何人来说都是不可行的方式，他们对我的容忍度会迅速下降。在大多数情况下，我会写下我的理解，就像我与主要来源隔离一样，制作闪存卡并积极练习我所学到的知识，只是更有能力询问我难以理解或需要澄清的要点。

sentences.en: Effectively, I am doing the following in a capacity I personally never had via any other means:> I seem to be doing a lot of integrating and reorganizing my thoughts.If you are using it as a slot machine of knowledge, and going from receiving > doing with no intermediate step I see how outcomes could differ.
sentences.zh: 实际上，我正在以我个人从未通过任何其他方式获得的能力做以下事情：>我似乎正在做很多整合和重组我的想法。如果你将它用作知识的老虎机，并且从接收>做没有中间步骤，我会看到结果会有什么不同。

blocks.html_en: Seconded. At a minimum, you can literally tell the agent to guide you but not point you and only help when you are stuck if you're actively trying to learn something. It's been a help for me to bridge a couple of gaps I'd struggled to cross before (mainly hardware things).

blocks.html_en:
> This sounds like it's more down to how the individual uses the tool.

I genuinely agree with a lot of your comment but lines like this drive me crazy. Anytime somebody has a critique of AI, so many people hand wave away with “well you’re doing it wrong.” If we can just chalk up every negative thing with AI to “incorrect tool usage” then we’ll never honestly assess it.

sentences.en: > This sounds like it's more down to how the individual uses the tool.I genuinely agree with a lot of your comment but lines like this drive me crazy.
sentences.zh: > 这听起来更像是个人如何使用该工具。我真的同意你的很多评​​论，但这样的台词让我发疯。

sentences.en: Anytime somebody has a critique of AI, so many people hand wave away with “well you’re doing it wrong.”
sentences.zh: 每当有人对人工智能提出批评时，很多人都会表示“好吧，你做错了”。

sentences.en: If we can just chalk up every negative thing with AI to “incorrect tool usage” then we’ll never honestly assess it.
sentences.zh: 如果我们只能将人工智能的每一个负面因素归结为“不正确的工具使用”，那么我们永远不会诚实地评估它。

sentences.en: I genuinely agree with a lot of your comment but lines like this drive me crazy.
sentences.zh: 我真的同意你的很多评​​论，但这样的台词让我发疯。

sentences.en: Anytime somebody has a critique of AI, so many people hand wave away with “well you’re doing it wrong.”
sentences.zh: 每当有人对人工智能提出批评时，很多人都会表示“好吧，你做错了”。

sentences.en: If we can just chalk up every negative thing with AI to “incorrect tool usage” then we’ll never honestly assess it.
sentences.zh: 如果我们只能将人工智能的每一个负面因素归结为“不正确的工具使用”，那么我们永远不会诚实地评估它。

blocks.html_en:
I didn’t say they were using AI “wrong.” That’s a stronger claim than the one I made.

Their assessment was:

> When interacting with LLMs it feels a lot more like I'm just receiving knowledge passively and I don't think it gets integrated as well.

My point is that the passivity is not necessarily an inherent property of the tool. It depends heavily on an individual chooses to engage with the output.

Saying LLMs make learning passive is a bit like someone else saying books make learning passive because you can skim them without thinking. When we all know instead of reading a book passively you could in addition annotate it, question its claims, summarise it, test yourself, and ultimately apply what you learned. The medium permits both. The same is true of the output of an LLM. You'll get out what you put in and nothing more.

You can use it like a slot machine, repeatedly pulling the lever for another answer without reflecting on any of them. But the fact that a tool permits shallow engagement does not mean shallow engagement is the only option available to you.

That also doesn’t mean every criticism of AI should be dismissed as “incorrect tool usage.” AI has real limitations and deserves serious criticism, and I assure you, I am not popular with the AI hype bros for this reason. However we still need to distinguish between limitations of the tool and consequences of how someone uses it. Not every potentially negative experience is rooted entirely in the technology itself. Sometimes user behaviour is a significant part of the cause.

sentences.en: I didn’t say they were using AI “wrong.”
sentences.zh: 我并没有说他们使用人工智能“错误”。

sentences.en: That’s a stronger claim than the one I made.Their assessment was:> When interacting with LLMs it feels a lot more like I'm just receiving knowledge passively and I don't think it gets integrated as well.My point is that the passivity is not necessarily an inherent property of the tool.
sentences.zh: 这是一个比我提出的更有力的主张。他们的评估是：>当与法学硕士互动时，感觉更像是我只是被动地接收知识，而且我认为它也没有得到整合。我的观点是，被动性不一定是该工具的固有属性。

sentences.en: It depends heavily on an individual chooses to engage with the output.Saying LLMs make learning passive is a bit like someone else saying books make learning passive because you can skim them without thinking.
sentences.zh: 这在很大程度上取决于个人选择参与产出。说法学硕士让学习变得被动，有点像其他人说书籍让学习变得被动，因为你可以不假思索地浏览它们。

sentences.en: When we all know instead of reading a book passively you could in addition annotate it, question its claims, summarise it, test yourself, and ultimately apply what you learned.
sentences.zh: 当我们都知道而不是被动地阅读一本书时，你还可以对其进行注释，质疑其主张，总结它，测试自己，并最终应用你所学到的知识。

sentences.en: The medium permits both.
sentences.zh: 媒介允许两者。

sentences.en: The same is true of the output of an LLM.
sentences.zh: LLM 的输出也是如此。

sentences.en: You'll get out what you put in and nothing more.You can use it like a slot machine, repeatedly pulling the lever for another answer without reflecting on any of them.
sentences.zh: 你会得到你投入的东西，仅此而已。你可以像老虎机一样使用它，反复拉动杠杆以获得另一个答案，而无需考虑其中任何一个。

sentences.en: But the fact that a tool permits shallow engagement does not mean shallow engagement is the only option available to you.That also doesn’t mean every criticism of AI should be dismissed as “incorrect tool usage.”
sentences.zh: 但工具允许浅层参与的事实并不意味着浅层参与是你唯一可用的选择。这也不意味着对人工智能的每一次批评都应该被视为“不正确的工具使用”。

sentences.en: AI has real limitations and deserves serious criticism, and I assure you, I am not popular with the AI hype bros for this reason.
sentences.zh: 人工智能有真正的局限性，值得严厉批评，我向你保证，因此我不喜欢人工智能炒作兄弟。

sentences.en: However we still need to distinguish between limitations of the tool and consequences of how someone uses it.
sentences.zh: 然而，我们仍然需要区分该工具的局限性和人们如何使用它的后果。

sentences.en: Not every potentially negative experience is rooted entirely in the technology itself.
sentences.zh: 并非所有潜在的负面体验都完全源于技术本身。

sentences.en: Sometimes user behaviour is a significant part of the cause.
sentences.zh: 有时，用户行为是原因的重要组成部分。

Any such bet would be grossly misguided given how different people acquire information differently. Just because you don't like using LLMs doesn't automatically extrapolate to a general realisation about the human brain or how one approaches knowledge.

blocks.html_en:
Thank you for sharing this.

I do think book prizes are a better-than-average signal but I previously volunteered with a book award. I will caution that pretty much every publisher mass-submits these books for consideration in every remotely relevant prize. It is a cost of doing business (similar to how photographers pay to enter photography competitions to try and win the "award winning photographer" title, or businesses submit dossiers with consideration fees on why they're one of Michigan's top 100 places to work).

There are often so many books and so few willing qualified readers that which books get an award can either be completely arbitrary, or comically easy.

For example, the NCR Book Award in your book corpus faced a big scandal when it was revealed that the judges did not read the books themselves. [1] The PROSE award is so comically large that an ordinary category finalist or win is usually overstated in prestige and value.

[1] https://www.theguardian.com/news/2013/may/19/literary-prize-...

sentences.en: Thank you for sharing this.I do think book prizes are a better-than-average signal but I previously volunteered with a book award.
sentences.zh: 感谢您分享这一点。我确实认为图书奖是一个优于平均水平的信号，但我之前自愿获得了图书奖。

sentences.en: I will caution that pretty much every publisher mass-submits these books for consideration in every remotely relevant prize.
sentences.zh: 我要提醒大家的是，几乎每个出版商都会大量提交这些书籍，以供每个相关奖项考虑。

sentences.en: It is a cost of doing business (similar to how photographers pay to enter photography competitions to try and win the "award winning photographer" title, or businesses submit dossiers with consideration fees on why they're one of Michigan's top 100 places to work).There are often so many books and so few willing qualified readers that which books get an award can either be completely arbitrary, or comically easy.For example, the NCR Book Award in your book corpus faced a big scandal when it was revealed that the judges did not read the books themselves. [1] The PROSE award is so comically large that an ordinary category finalist or win is usually overstated in prestige and value.[1] https://www.theguardian.com/news/2013/may/19/literary-prize-...
sentences.zh: 这是做生意的成本（类似于摄影师花钱参加摄影比赛，试图赢得“获奖摄影师”称号，或者企业提交带有考虑费的档案，解释为什么他们是密歇根州前 100 个最佳工作场所之一）。通常有这么多的书，而愿意的合格读者却很少，导致哪些书获得奖项可能是完全任意的，或者是滑稽地容易。例如，当你的图书语料库中的 NCR 图书奖被揭露时，它就面临着一场大丑闻。评委们自己并没有读过这些书。 [1] PROSE 奖的规模如此之大，以至于普通类别的决赛入围者或获胜者的声望和价值通常被夸大了。[1] https://www.theguardian.com/news/2013/may/19/literary-prize-...

It is a cost of doing business (similar to how photographers pay to enter photography competitions to try and win the "award winning photographer" title, or businesses submit dossiers with consideration fees on why they're one of Michigan's top 100 places to work).

sentences.en: I do think book prizes are a better-than-average signal but I previously volunteered with a book award.
sentences.zh: 我确实认为图书奖是一个好于平均水平的信号，但我之前自愿获得了图书奖。

sentences.en: I will caution that pretty much every publisher mass-submits these books for consideration in every remotely relevant prize.
sentences.zh: 我要提醒大家的是，几乎每个出版商都会大量提交这些书籍，以供每个相关奖项考虑。

sentences.en: It is a cost of doing business (similar to how photographers pay to enter photography competitions to try and win the "award winning photographer" title, or businesses submit dossiers with consideration fees on why they're one of Michigan's top 100 places to work).
sentences.zh: 这是开展业务的成本（类似于摄影师付费参加摄影比赛以尝试赢得“获奖摄影师”称号，或者企业提交包含考虑费的档案，说明为什么他们是密歇根州 100 家最佳工作场所之一）。

blocks.html_en: There are often so many books and so few willing qualified readers that which books get an award can either be completely arbitrary, or comically easy.

sentences.en: There are often so many books and so few willing qualified readers that which books get an award can either be completely arbitrary, or comically easy.
sentences.zh: 通常，书籍数量如此之多，而愿意的合格读者却如此之少，以至于哪些书籍获得奖项要么完全是任意的，要么是简单得可笑。

blocks.html_en: For example, the NCR Book Award in your book corpus faced a big scandal when it was revealed that the judges did not read the books themselves. [1] The PROSE award is so comically large that an ordinary category finalist or win is usually overstated in prestige and value.

sentences.en: For example, the NCR Book Award in your book corpus faced a big scandal when it was revealed that the judges did not read the books themselves. [1] The PROSE award is so comically large that an ordinary category finalist or win is usually overstated in prestige and value.
sentences.zh: 例如，您的图书语料库中的 NCR 图书奖面临着一场大丑闻，因为评委们自己没有读过这些书。 [1] PROSE 奖的数额如此之​​大，以至于普通的决赛入围者或获胜者的声望和价值通常被夸大了。

blocks.html_en: [1] https://www.theguardian.com/news/2013/may/19/literary-prize-...

sentences.en: [1] https://www.theguardian.com/news/2013/may/19/literary-prize-...

blocks.html_en: Awards are also a product of their time, representing biases among the judges, literary preferences from the readership, and straight up nepotism/favoritisim/elitism. Look at the Hugo/Nebula/Locus and you'll find a lot of so-so winning work throughout the years; especially when you look at the other nominees.

sentences.en: Awards are also a product of their time, representing biases among the judges, literary preferences from the readership, and straight up nepotism/favoritisim/elitism.
sentences.zh: 奖项也是时代的产物，代表了评委之间的偏见、读者的文学偏好以及直接的裙带关系/偏袒主义/精英主义。

sentences.en: Look at the Hugo/Nebula/Locus and you'll find a lot of so-so winning work throughout the years; especially when you look at the other nominees.
sentences.zh: 看看Hugo/Nebula/Locus，你会发现这些年来有很多马马虎虎的获奖作品；尤其是当你看看其他提名者时。

blocks.html_en:
If you are interested in a non-fiction book, check out Veritas: Truth Across Cultures[1]. It's a collection of quotes/saying/proverbs that are similar between cultures, which I call truths. I am in interested in long term thinking, things that stood the test of time. This was primarily put together for myself as a lens to view the world.

[1] https://www.chestergrant.com/veritas

blocks.html_en:
Thank you for this. I sorted the "technology" and "science" sections and saw a few excellent books that I've read and a few that I would really like to read. This motivates me to start setting aside "reading time" again every day, since I've lost that habit.

The recent "society & culture" books gave me some good book club ideas.

Bug report: filtering by "award" appears to be broken for some awards. If I select Pulitzer or National Book Award, no books show up, but I can find books with these awards by browsing.

sentences.en: Thank you for this.
sentences.zh: 谢谢你。

sentences.zh: 我对“技术”和“科学”部分进行了分类，看到了一些我读过的优秀书籍和一些我非常想读的书籍。

blocks.html_en:
One thing that would make this information even more useful and searchable would be a JSON dump of your dataset. You could serve it statically on S3 or similar, to keep hosting costs to a minimum.

Of course, having created a cool thing generates no obligation for further work on your part! I'm grateful that you did this work and made it available for free.

Did you build your semantic search index based on the full text of the books, or just the reviews and descriptions?

blocks.html_en:
I recently did this! I fixed my phone habit and my TV habit, and I just read no, the family has silent reading parties instead of movie night.

Here’s what I did: 1. Install Jomo on the phone. There are other apps, but basically it makes me wait 5s before I can open a brainrot app, and then I can only use it for 5min, and every time I do this I have to wait an additional 5s. It resets at midnight. This introduces the necessary friction.

2. Paper books. The house is now full of paper books. Book seems interesting? Buy it. Books everywhere. Real books.

3. The actual habit. I stack habits. Right now I have a daily workout routine. First thing I leave the house and either run or go to the gym. I bring my book, and afterwards go to the cafe across the street for a coffee. At night, I read before going to bed. Once you finish a few books this way, you’re in. TV now is actually hard to pay attention to, and I feel kind of… dirty doomscrolling now (like eating junk food, it tastes good but you feel gross during the experience).

If you want something to start with: https://bookshop.org/p/books/true-grit-charles-portis/5ac454...

sentences.en: I recently did this!
sentences.zh: 我最近做了这个！

sentences.en: I fixed my phone habit and my TV habit, and I just read no, the family has silent reading parties instead of movie night.Here’s what I did: 1.
sentences.zh: 我改掉了看手机的习惯和看电视的习惯，我只是读到“不，家里有无声读书会，而不是电影之夜”。这就是我所做的：1。

sentences.en: Install Jomo on the phone.
sentences.zh: 在手机上安装 Jomo。

blocks.html_en:
I recently did this! I fixed my phone habit and my TV habit, and I just read no, the family has silent reading parties instead of movie night.

Here’s what I did: 1. Install Jomo on the phone. There are other apps, but basically it makes me wait 5s before I can open a brainrot app, and then I can only use it for 5min, and every time I do this I have to wait an additional 5s. It resets at midnight. This introduces the necessary friction.

2. Paper books. The house is now full of paper books. Book seems interesting? Buy it. Books everywhere. Real books.

3. The actual habit. I stack habits. Right now I have a daily workout routine. First thing I leave the house and either run or go to the gym. I bring my book, and afterwards go to the cafe across the street for a coffee. At night, I read before going to bed. Once you finish a few books this way, you’re in. TV now is actually hard to pay attention to, and I feel kind of… dirty doomscrolling now (like eating junk food, it tastes good but you feel gross during the experience).

If you want something to start with: https://bookshop.org/p/books/true-grit-charles-portis/5ac454...

blocks.html_en:
I bought a tiny e-reader [0] and carry that as well as my phone. When the temptation to doomscroll triggers, I pull out the e-reader instead and read a book.

I tried attaching the book to the phone as their marketing suggests, but that didn't click somehow. Having both out means I would check the phone and then not look at the book.

Working so far, but it's only been a couple of weeks, so let's see.

[0] after reading this HN post: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48662381

blocks.html_en:
Yea, for me my Kindle just didnt work. I always have had one since they were first released, but it was always hard to keep the habit between book.

Having a nice library of paper books, for whatever reason, has worked a lot better for me. I think it’s passively seeing them around the house and in the background always considering what is next.

This last round I couldn’t decide what to dig in to after I’d finished True Grit, so I spent a few minutes browsing what we had on hand and it was just… a very pleasant experience.

Also, unless you pirate ebooks or have access to a library that offers them, used paperbacks are actually much cheaper than buying ebooks.

sentences.en: Yea, for me my Kindle just didnt work.
sentences.zh: 是的，对我来说，我的 Kindle 无法使用。

sentences.en: I always have had one since they were first released, but it was always hard to keep the habit between book.Having a nice library of paper books, for whatever reason, has worked a lot better for me.
sentences.zh: 自从它们第一次发布以来，我一直都有一本，但在书之间保持这个习惯总是很难。无论出于何种原因，拥有一个漂亮的纸质书籍图书馆对我来说效果更好。

sentences.en: I think it’s passively seeing them around the house and in the background always considering what is next.This last round I couldn’t decide what to dig in to after I’d finished True Grit, so I spent a few minutes browsing what we had on hand and it was just…
sentences.zh: 我认为这是被动地看到他们在房子周围和在后台总是考虑接下来会发生什么。最后一轮我在完成《大地惊雷》后无法决定要深入研究什么，所以我花了几分钟浏览我们手头的东西，这只是……

sentences.en: a very pleasant experience.Also, unless you pirate ebooks or have access to a library that offers them, used paperbacks are actually much cheaper than buying ebooks.
sentences.zh: 一次非常愉快的体验。此外，除非您盗版电子书或可以访问提供电子书的图书馆，否则二手平装书实际上比购买电子书便宜得多。

blocks.html_en: Thanks for sharing jomo. My hack has been to read on my kindle while standing or even walking short repetitive steps (in surroundings or patterns where I can't hurt myself). This has been a game changer while waiting for kids soccer games to get done, lines in the mall, baggage claim etc. It also helps me avoid sedentary reading while sitting.

sentences.en: Thanks for sharing jomo.
sentences.zh: 感谢分享乔莫。

sentences.en: My hack has been to read on my kindle while standing or even walking short repetitive steps (in surroundings or patterns where I can't hurt myself).
sentences.zh: 我的秘诀是在我的kindle上阅读，同时站立，甚至短距离重复行走（在我不会伤害自己的环境或模式中）。

sentences.en: This has been a game changer while waiting for kids soccer games to get done, lines in the mall, baggage claim etc. It also helps me avoid sedentary reading while sitting.
sentences.zh: 在等待儿童足球比赛结束、在商场排队、领取行李等过程中，这改变了游戏规则。它还帮助我避免坐着时久坐阅读。

This has been a game changer while waiting for kids soccer games to get done, lines in the mall, baggage claim etc. It also helps me avoid sedentary reading while sitting.

sentences.en: Thanks for sharing jomo.

sentences.zh: 我的秘诀是在我的kindle上阅读，同时站立，甚至短距离重复行走（在我不会伤害自己的环境或模式中）。

I clicked a few categories and the top ranked books all had the “popularity contest” smell, sort of the antithesis of random exploration.

blocks.html_en:
Original author/creator of the site here - thank you! Will add the Axiom one. I am on the fence about whether/how to add "book of the year" type lists as they are somewhat distinct from book awards, but I do think that would probably be the next step to get more books in the corpus.

And any other ideas that HN readers have for awards to add would be welcome. Currently it's probably too history-slanted since I'm a historian and knew those awards better.

sentences.en: Will add the Axiom one.
sentences.zh: 将添加 Axiom 之一。

sentences.en: I am on the fence about whether/how to add "book of the year" type lists as they are somewhat distinct from book awards, but I do think that would probably be the next step to get more books in the corpus.And any other ideas that HN readers have for awards to add would be welcome.

sentences.zh: 目前它可能太偏向历史了，因为我是一名历史学家并且更了解这些奖项。

blocks.html_en:
It may be hard to parse the lists but if they're still available somewhere online under the current administration the past annual State Department, CIA bookshelf recommendation, Army Chief of Staff, Navy CNO and Marine Commandant reading lists may have some valuable additions. To my knowledge there aren't any analogues to them in the rest of the Anglosphere but some LLMs will probably point out ones I haven't thought of.

I can point out that the UN agencies' respective reading lists for their staffs' professional development are 99% internal UN papers of very limited interest to external audiences but I haven't had a professional reason to look at any EU agencies to confirm or deny value.

Additionally, the Financial Times has a second set of book awards along their main awards which you've not listed, the FT reader's best books list.

sentences.en: It may be hard to parse the lists but if they're still available somewhere online under the current administration the past annual State Department, CIA bookshelf recommendation, Army Chief of Staff, Navy CNO and Marine Commandant reading lists may have some valuable additions.
sentences.zh: 解析这些清单可能很困难，但如果在现任政府的领导下，它们仍然可以在网上找到，过去的年度国务院、中央情报局书架推荐、陆军参谋长、海军首席网络官和海军陆战队司令的阅读清单可能会增加一些有价值的内容。

sentences.en: To my knowledge there aren't any analogues to them in the rest of the Anglosphere but some LLMs will probably point out ones I haven't thought of.I can point out that the UN agencies' respective reading lists for their staffs' professional development are 99% internal UN papers of very limited interest to external audiences but I haven't had a professional reason to look at any EU agencies to confirm or deny value.Additionally, the Financial Times has a second set of book awards along their main awards which you've not listed, the FT reader's best books list.
sentences.zh: 据我所知，在英语圈的其他地方没有任何类似的东西，但一些法学硕士可能会指出我没有想到的。我可以指出，联合国机构各自为其工作人员专业发展制定的阅读清单99％是联合国内部论文，对外部读者的兴趣非常有限，但我没有专业的理由去查看任何欧盟机构来确认或否认价值。此外，英国《金融时报》除了主要奖项之外还有第二套图书奖，这是你没有的列出英国《金融时报》读者最佳书籍排行榜。

blocks.html_en:
The war by itself is a far less interesting affair than how it happened, and what made the country be OK with fighting. For that you need to start at the end of the previous dictatorship, and cover the entire 2nd republic. Then the war starts making sense.

So I'd read The Collapse of the Spanish Republic, by Stanley Payne. He was a journalist in Spain through the entire period, so most of what he does is provide a first hand account. He never claims to try to be impartial, but he's pretty nouanced, as he knew a lot of the players personally.

sentences.en: The war by itself is a far less interesting affair than how it happened, and what made the country be OK with fighting.
sentences.zh: 战争本身并不是一件有趣的事情，重要的是它是如何发生的，以及是什么让这个国家能够接受战争。

sentences.en: For that you need to start at the end of the previous dictatorship, and cover the entire 2nd republic.
sentences.zh: 为此，你需要从前一个独裁统治结束时开始，覆盖整个第二共和国。

sentences.en: Then the war starts making sense.So I'd read The Collapse of the Spanish Republic, by Stanley Payne.
sentences.zh: 然后战争就开始有意义了。所以我读了斯坦利·佩恩的《西班牙共和国的崩溃》。

sentences.en: He was a journalist in Spain through the entire period, so most of what he does is provide a first hand account.
sentences.zh: 整个时期他都是西班牙的一名记者，所以他所做的大部分工作就是提供第一手资料。

sentences.en: He never claims to try to be impartial, but he's pretty nouanced, as he knew a lot of the players personally.
sentences.zh: 他从来没有声称要做到公正，但他非常谨慎，因为他私下认识很多球员。

blocks.html_en: The war by itself is a far less interesting affair than how it happened, and what made the country be OK with fighting. For that you need to start at the end of the previous dictatorship, and cover the entire 2nd republic. Then the war starts making sense.

sentences.en: The war by itself is a far less interesting affair than how it happened, and what made the country be OK with fighting.
sentences.zh: 战争本身并不是一件有趣的事情，重要的是它是如何发生的，以及是什么让这个国家能够接受战争。

sentences.en: For that you need to start at the end of the previous dictatorship, and cover the entire 2nd republic.
sentences.zh: 为此，你需要从前一个独裁统治结束时开始，覆盖整个第二共和国。

sentences.en: Then the war starts making sense.
sentences.zh: 然后战争就开始有意义了。

blocks.html_en: So I'd read The Collapse of the Spanish Republic, by Stanley Payne. He was a journalist in Spain through the entire period, so most of what he does is provide a first hand account. He never claims to try to be impartial, but he's pretty nouanced, as he knew a lot of the players personally.

sentences.en: So I'd read The Collapse of the Spanish Republic, by Stanley Payne.
sentences.zh: 所以我读了斯坦利·佩恩的《西班牙共和国的崩溃》。

sentences.en: He was a journalist in Spain through the entire period, so most of what he does is provide a first hand account.
sentences.zh: 整个时期他都是西班牙的一名记者，所以他所做的大部分工作就是提供第一手资料。

sentences.en: He never claims to try to be impartial, but he's pretty nouanced, as he knew a lot of the players personally.
sentences.zh: 他从来没有声称要做到公正，但他非常谨慎，因为他私下认识很多球员。

Books on the Spanish Civil War I have read and can wholeheartedly recommend:

- "The Spanish Civil War" by Paul Preston

- "Homage to Catalonia" by George Orwell

Books on the same topic on my reading list (and quite frankly can't wait to get hold of them):

- "The Spanish Civil War" by Hugh Thomas

- "The Fight for Spain" by Antony Beevor

blocks.html_en:
Paul Preston's "The Spanish Civil War" would be the poster child of the "quality non-fiction" category specified in this post's title. He is, in my opinion, the leading Hispanist historian [1], and I hold his work in the highest esteem. I can also very much recommend his book "A People Betrayed".

Regarding the other two authors in my previous comment: Hugh Thomas was also a leading Hispanist historian [2], and Antony Beevor is a prominent military historian [3]. I expect both books to be in the same vein as Preston's.

(Quick aside for the astute reader that noticed all of the recommended authors being non-Spanish: I find Spanish politics extremely polarized; the Spanish Civil War being perhaps the most polarizing topic. As such, I find outsider's perspectives to be more balanced and objective.)

References:

1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paul_Preston

2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugh_Thomas,_Baron_Thomas_of_S...

3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antony_Beevor

sentences.zh: 在我看来，他是领先的西班牙历史学家[1]，我非常尊重他的工作。

sentences.en: I can also very much recommend his book "A People Betrayed".Regarding the other two authors in my previous comment: Hugh Thomas was also a leading Hispanist historian [2], and Antony Beevor is a prominent military historian [3].
sentences.zh: 我也非常推荐他的书《被背叛的人》。关于我之前评论中的另外两位作者：休·托马斯也是一位领先的西班牙历史学家[2]，安东尼·比沃是一位著名的军事历史学家[3]。

sentences.en: I expect both books to be in the same vein as Preston's.(Quick aside for the astute reader that noticed all of the recommended authors being non-Spanish: I find Spanish politics extremely polarized; the Spanish Civil War being perhaps the most polarizing topic.
sentences.zh: 我希望这两本书与普雷斯顿的风格相同。（对于那些注意到所有推荐的作者都不是西班牙人的精明读者来说，快速说一句：我发现西班牙政治极其两极分化；西班牙内战也许是最两极分化的话题。

sentences.en: As such, I find outsider's perspectives to be more balanced and objective.)References:1.
sentences.zh: 因此，我觉得局外人的观点更加平衡和客观。）参考文献：1．
