blocks.html_en:
> Quick aside for the astute reader that noticed all of the recommended authors being non-Spanish: I find Spanish politics extremely polarized; the Spanish Civil War being perhaps the most polarizing topic. As such, I find outsider's perspectives to be more balanced and objective

Makes sense to me, even as someone who live in Spain. It's a hard subject to talk to people about, especially because it never really was addressed as a country, everyone (politicians) just agreed to pretend it never happened, sweep a bunch of stuff under the rug and hope for the best. Then everyone act surprised when things start to bubble again...

I think the approach makes sense, also why you probably don't want to read about US politics from people actively inside of US politics, and it's similarly polarized today. Seemingly, at some point, people just lose all head and reason, and just start backing into their side, regardless of what that means.

sentences.en: > Quick aside for the astute reader that noticed all of the recommended authors being non-Spanish: I find Spanish politics extremely polarized; the Spanish Civil War being perhaps the most polarizing topic.
sentences.zh: > 敏锐的读者注意到所有推荐的作者都不是西班牙人：我发现西班牙政治极其两极分化；西班牙内战也许是最两极分化的话题。

sentences.en: As such, I find outsider's perspectives to be more balanced and objectiveMakes sense to me, even as someone who live in Spain.
sentences.zh: 因此，我发现局外人的观点更加平衡和客观，对我来说很有意义，即使作为住在西班牙的人也是如此。

sentences.en: It's a hard subject to talk to people about, especially because it never really was addressed as a country, everyone (politicians) just agreed to pretend it never happened, sweep a bunch of stuff under the rug and hope for the best.
sentences.zh: 这是一个很难与人们谈论的话题，特别是因为它从未真正被视为一个国家，每个人（政客）都同意假装它从未发生过，把一堆东西隐藏起来，并希望有最好的结果。

sentences.en: Then everyone act surprised when things start to bubble again...I think the approach makes sense, also why you probably don't want to read about US politics from people actively inside of US politics, and it's similarly polarized today.
sentences.zh: 然后，当事情再次开始泡沫时，每个人都表现得感到惊讶……我认为这种方法是有道理的，也是为什么你可能不想从积极参与美国政治的人们那里读到美国政治，而且今天也同样两极分化。

sentences.en: Seemingly, at some point, people just lose all head and reason, and just start backing into their side, regardless of what that means.
sentences.zh: 似乎，在某些时候，人们会失去理智，开始倒向自己一边，不管这意味着什么。

blocks.html_en:
Not to be that guy but your site is terrible.

It does not do the most important function well, giving me a list of award winning books that is easy to sort through. It is not clear what is a link and what is not.

It looks fine though somewhat generic. But it is not actually functional in a real sense.

sentences.en: Not to be that guy but your site is terrible.It does not do the most important function well, giving me a list of award winning books that is easy to sort through.

sentences.zh: 目前尚不清楚什么是链接，什么不是。它看起来不错，但有些通用。

sentences.en: But it is not actually functional in a real sense.
sentences.zh: 但它实际上并没有真正意义上的功能。

blocks.html_en: This hits the nail on the head regarding the real utility of AI right now. The magic isn’t in using LLMs to generate endless reams of synthetic text; it’s in using embeddings and semantic search to navigate high-signal, human-curated data.

sentences.en: This hits the nail on the head regarding the real utility of AI right now.
sentences.zh: 这对于人工智能目前的真正用途来说是一针见血。

sentences.en: The magic isn’t in using LLMs to generate endless reams of synthetic text; it’s in using embeddings and semantic search to navigate high-signal, human-curated data.
sentences.zh: 神奇之处并不在于使用法学硕士生成无数的合成文本；而是在于使用法学硕士生成无数的合成文本。它使用嵌入和语义搜索来导航高信号、人工管理的数据。

blocks.html_en: Worth stating that this is one of the success stories from AI. Someone who has domain expertise outside of programming is able to create a really useful piece of software because the barrier to entry has been significantly lowered. Really nice.

sentences.en: Worth stating that this is one of the success stories from AI.
sentences.zh: 值得一提的是，这是人工智能的成功故事之一。

sentences.en: Someone who has domain expertise outside of programming is able to create a really useful piece of software because the barrier to entry has been significantly lowered.
sentences.zh: 拥有编程之外领域专业知识的人能够创建真正有用的软件，因为进入门槛已大大降低。

sentences.en: Really nice.
sentences.zh: 真的很好。

blocks.html_en:
On a related note, I was just noting to my co-founder, as we struggle to write good case studies for our website, that I find LLMs are astoundingly bad at writing good prose.

We all know the "AI-tics" that give away a sloppily AI-written piece, but even if you steer them, they still struggle to write consistently high-quality prose.

Somehow I feel that the work of a good copywriter has never been more noticeable.

sentences.en: On a related note, I was just noting to my co-founder, as we struggle to write good case studies for our website, that I find LLMs are astoundingly bad at writing good prose.We all know the "AI-tics" that give away a sloppily AI-written piece, but even if you steer them, they still struggle to write consistently high-quality prose.Somehow I feel that the work of a good copywriter has never been more noticeable.
sentences.zh: 与此相关的是，我只是向我的联合创始人指出，当我们努力为我们的网站撰写优秀的案例研究时，我发现法学硕士在撰写优秀的散文方面极其糟糕。我们都知道“AI-tics”会导致人工智能撰写的文章草率，但即使你引导他们，他们仍然很难写出始终如一的高质量散文。不知怎的，我觉得一个优秀的文案作家的工作从未如此引人注目。

blocks.html_en: I recently realized this as well and I think what I’ve discovered is that AI just produces mediocre content in all realms, but you don’t really notice it except in the realms where you have real expertise. With a lot of harness and prompting you can have it pump out something that’s pretty good but by default the next best token rarely produces anything of quality it seems like and if you think it does, perhaps you may want to recheck your assumptions on your expertise of the topic at hand

sentences.en: I recently realized this as well and I think what I’ve discovered is that AI just produces mediocre content in all realms, but you don’t really notice it except in the realms where you have real expertise.
sentences.zh: 我最近也意识到了这一点，我认为我发现人工智能在所有领域都只会产生平庸的内容，但除了在你拥有真正专业知识的领域之外，你不会真正注意到它。

sentences.en: With a lot of harness and prompting you can have it pump out something that’s pretty good but by default the next best token rarely produces anything of quality it seems like and if you think it does, perhaps you may want to recheck your assumptions on your expertise of the topic at hand
sentences.zh: 通过大量的利用和提示，你可以让它输出一些非常好的东西，但默认情况下，下一个最好的代币很少会产生看起来像的质量，如果你认为它确实如此，也许你可能需要重新检查你对你手头主题的专业知识的假设

blocks.html_en:
I do think this explains much of it. But it doesn’t explain why Claude often writes in the clipped tone of that gnomic in-house tech evangelist who was inexplicably hired because he impressed the CEO.

(I may be projecting real life experience onto the LLM.)

I am genuinely fascinated as to how Claude acquired its utterly aggravating way of writing. It’s so much more irritating than ChatGPT, which is already not good.

sentences.en: I do think this explains much of it.
sentences.zh: 我确实认为这可以解释很多事情。

sentences.en: But it doesn’t explain why Claude often writes in the clipped tone of that gnomic in-house tech evangelist who was inexplicably hired because he impressed the CEO.(I may be projecting real life experience onto the LLM.)I am genuinely fascinated as to how Claude acquired its utterly aggravating way of writing.
sentences.zh: 但这并不能解释为什么克劳德经常用那种精明的内部技术传播者的简洁语气来写作，他被莫名其妙地聘用，因为他给首席执行官留下了深刻的印象。（我可能将现实生活经验投射到法学硕士身上。）我真的很着迷于克劳德如何获得其完全令人恼火的写作方式。

sentences.en: It’s so much more irritating than ChatGPT, which is already not good.
sentences.zh: 比ChatGPT烦人太多了，已经不好了。

blocks.html_en: Yeah the latest batch is really keyed on certain phrases - well beyond “you’re absolutely right!” Of the past. And the readability is trash almost always by default. Probably because they are forcing code so hard that it’s aligning prose into functional groups or something.

sentences.zh: 过去的。

sentences.en: And the readability is trash almost always by default.
sentences.zh: 默认情况下，可读性几乎总是垃圾。

sentences.zh: 可能是因为他们对代码的要求如此之高，以至于将散文调整为功能组或其他东西。

blocks.html_en:
Random observation: Google's Gemma 4 models write so much nicer prose than ChatGPT or Claude.

Though this might be me as a British reader, simply preferring a rather less American turn of phrase.

I reckon the more transatlantic, english-as-international language DeepMind team have had a subliminal (or maybe deliberate) impact on the way it chooses to write.

Or perhaps small open weights models simply aren't under the same commercial pressure to be engaging and sycophantic and are therefore less likely to adopt the samey overly casual, upbeat, Californian sales assistant manner. (Don't get me wrong, I like this from real human Californians just fine!)

Either way, the default tone is much less showy. I would be interested to find out if you agree.

I am very much an LLM cynic. I am engaging because I must, and trying to learn fundamentals, but I would not say I am overly excited by any of this, just glad that small open weights models exist as a counterpoint.

I loathe the way ChatGPT writes, and the Claude-isms that are everywhere; it is actually quite enraging, especially when you start seeing it in internet comments from people who used to try to write out their own thoughts.

But in my experiments with open weights models I have found I am much less aggravated by summaries and outlines written by Gemma 4, so much that I am happy enough to read them, because they have fewer irritants that take me out of the reading flow.

Though this evening it told me very kindly that my photography is a bit "safe". How very dare it… understand me that well.

sentences.en: Random observation: Google's Gemma 4 models write so much nicer prose than ChatGPT or Claude.Though this might be me as a British reader, simply preferring a rather less American turn of phrase.I reckon the more transatlantic, english-as-international language DeepMind team have had a subliminal (or maybe deliberate) impact on the way it chooses to write.Or perhaps small open weights models simply aren't under the same commercial pressure to be engaging and sycophantic and are therefore less likely to adopt the samey overly casual, upbeat, Californian sales assistant manner.
sentences.zh: 随机观察：Google 的 Gemma 4 模型写的散文比 ChatGPT 或 Claude 好得多。虽然这可能是我作为一名英国读者，只是更喜欢不太美国式的措辞。我认为更跨大西洋、英语作为国际语言的 DeepMind 团队对其选择的写作方式产生了潜意识（或者可能是故意）的影响。或者也许小型开放权重模型根本没有受到同样的商业压力来吸引和参与谄媚的人，因此不太可能采用同样过于随意、乐观的加州销售助理方式。

sentences.en: (Don't get me wrong, I like this from real human Californians just fine!)Either way, the default tone is much less showy.
sentences.zh: （不要误会我的意思，我很喜欢来自真实的加利福尼亚人的这种感觉！）无论哪种方式，默认的语气都不那么华丽。

sentences.en: I would be interested to find out if you agree.I am very much an LLM cynic.
sentences.zh: 我很想知道你是否同意。我是一个非常愤世嫉俗的法学硕士。

sentences.en: I am engaging because I must, and trying to learn fundamentals, but I would not say I am overly excited by any of this, just glad that small open weights models exist as a counterpoint.I loathe the way ChatGPT writes, and the Claude-isms that are everywhere; it is actually quite enraging, especially when you start seeing it in internet comments from people who used to try to write out their own thoughts.But in my experiments with open weights models I have found I am much less aggravated by summaries and outlines written by Gemma 4, so much that I am happy enough to read them, because they have fewer irritants that take me out of the reading flow.Though this evening it told me very kindly that my photography is a bit "safe".
sentences.zh: 我很投入，因为我必须这样做，并努力学习基础知识，但我不会说我对这一切感到过度兴奋，只是很高兴小型开放权重模型作为对立面的存在。我讨厌 ChatGPT 的写作方式，以及无处不在的克劳德主义；这实际上是相当令人愤怒的，尤其是当你开始在互联网上看到那些曾经试图写出自己的想法的人的评论时。但在我对开放权重模型的实验中，我发现 Gemma 4 写的摘要和大纲没有那么让我恼火，以至于我很高兴阅读它们，因为它们没有太多让我脱离阅读流程的刺激物。尽管今晚它非常友善地告诉我，我的摄影有点“安全”。

sentences.en: How very dare it…
sentences.zh: 多么敢啊……

sentences.en: understand me that well.
sentences.zh: 这么理解我。

blocks.html_en:
I gave Claude Fable $25 in Pangram API credits and, after hundreds of attempts, it was unable to produce a single readable original piece of writing that was not immediately identified as AI.

This seems to be a hard problem for LLMs, as passing would probably require good self-perception ("oh no, I am writing like an AI!") and fine-grained control over its own output ("let's write like a human instead!").

sentences.en: I gave Claude Fable $25 in Pangram API credits and, after hundreds of attempts, it was unable to produce a single readable original piece of writing that was not immediately identified as AI.This seems to be a hard problem for LLMs, as passing would probably require good self-perception ("oh no, I am writing like an AI!") and fine-grained control over its own output ("let's write like a human instead!").
sentences.zh: 我给了 Claude Fable 25 美元的 Pangram API 学分，经过数百次尝试，它无法生成一篇可读的原创文章，并且没有立即被识别为人工智能。这对于法学硕士来说似乎是一个难题，因为通过可能需要良好的自我感知（“哦不，我像人工智能一样写作！”）和对其自身输出的细粒度控制（“让我们像人类一样写作！”）。

blocks.html_en:
I wonder if it partially because "write like a human" is kind of a vacuous request. Like, it's the objective everyone including me has been saying that we want, but there's no one way to write like a human and and humans don't even have a good definition past "I know it when I see it."

There's a lot of work in the humanities about different aspects of good writing, but that's not quite the same thing. And anyway they tend to assume a pre-existing level of writing ability. Students are supposed to learn good writing through practice; there are rules and exercises but they're incomplete.

sentences.en: I wonder if it partially because "write like a human" is kind of a vacuous request.
sentences.zh: 我想知道这是否部分是因为“像人一样写作”是一种空洞的要求。

sentences.zh: 就像，这是包括我在内的每个人都在说我们想要的目标，但没有一种方法可以像人类一样写作，而且人类甚至没有一个好的定义，除了“我看到它就知道它”。人文学科中有很多关于好写作的不同方面的工作，但这并不完全相同。

sentences.zh: 学生应该通过练习来学习良好的写作；有规则和练习，但它们并不完整。

sentences.zh: 就像，这是包括我在内的每个人都在说我们想要的目标，但没有一种方法可以像人类一样写作，而且人类甚至没有一个好的定义，除了“我看到它就知道它”。人文学科中有很多关于好写作的不同方面的工作，但这并不完全相同。

sentences.en: And anyway they tend to assume a pre-existing level of writing ability.
sentences.zh: 不管怎样，他们倾向于假设自己已经具备一定的写作能力。

sentences.en: Students are supposed to learn good writing through practice; there are rules and exercises but they're incomplete.
sentences.zh: 学生应该通过练习来学习良好的写作；有规则和练习，但它们并不完整。

Like, it's the objective everyone including me has been saying that we want, but there's no one way to write like a human and and humans don't even have a good definition past "I know it when I see it."

sentences.en: And anyway they tend to assume a pre-existing level of writing ability.

blocks.html_en:
I think as much it is that people write by grappling for the right phrase to represent some inner feeling or concept, writing in part for themselves, whereas LLMs write always and only for an audience.

It’s much easier to understand this once you think about other generative forms. MidJourney never just sits down and draws for fun, so fun never informs its art (only the outward appearance of others’ fun, separate from the fun itself). Suno doesn’t waste hours trying to find riffs on a guitar, so its output is never informed by the direct joy of getting it right. Its music is never optimised for playability on a particular guitar with a scratchy seventh fret and a too-high action. Neither Midjourney nor Suno have evolved their styles due to short-sightedness or carpal tunnel.

If you had a human writer who over a long career only ever wrote articles from an outline given to them by someone else, and you had all the outlines and all the resulting articles from those outlines, and you could train an LLM to generate an article from an outline, it still would not be kicking itself frustrated by an inelegant phrase in a prior article, it would not avoid certain phrases out of a passive aggressive reaction to some editor’s note, it would not ever just rush an article because everyone is gathering at the pub, and it would not choose an analogy just to rub the author of a bitchy critical letter to the editor the wrong way. An LLM could not “subtweet”. It could not write a series of articles hoping one important person will spot that they are auditioning for a job.

Creators have unseen, undocumented influences and motivations that inform their work over a long period. I don’t mean to say that these individual influences can be reliably detected in individual pieces of work. I do mean to say that I think their broad absence tends to be felt in LLM writing. As readers we develop an affinity for writers as much as for their writing, and we do this in part because we deduce things about them.

sentences.en: I think as much it is that people write by grappling for the right phrase to represent some inner feeling or concept, writing in part for themselves, whereas LLMs write always and only for an audience.It’s much easier to understand this once you think about other generative forms.
sentences.zh: 我认为，人们写作时会努力寻找正确的短语来代表某种内心的感受或概念，部分地为自己写作，而法学硕士总是且只为观众写作。一旦你考虑其他生成形式，就更容易理解这一点。

sentences.en: MidJourney never just sits down and draws for fun, so fun never informs its art (only the outward appearance of others’ fun, separate from the fun itself).
sentences.zh: MidJourney 从来不会只是为了好玩而坐下来画画，所以乐趣永远不会影响它的艺术（只是别人乐趣的外表，与乐趣本身分开）。

sentences.en: Suno doesn’t waste hours trying to find riffs on a guitar, so its output is never informed by the direct joy of getting it right.
sentences.zh: Suno 不会浪费时间去寻找吉他上的即兴重复段，因此它的输出从来不会因为正确而直接的喜悦而受到影响。

sentences.en: Its music is never optimised for playability on a particular guitar with a scratchy seventh fret and a too-high action.
sentences.zh: 它的音乐从未针对特定吉他的可演奏性进行优化，该吉他具有刺耳的七品和过高的击弦机。

sentences.en: Neither Midjourney nor Suno have evolved their styles due to short-sightedness or carpal tunnel.If you had a human writer who over a long career only ever wrote articles from an outline given to them by someone else, and you had all the outlines and all the resulting articles from those outlines, and you could train an LLM to generate an article from an outline, it still would not be kicking itself frustrated by an inelegant phrase in a prior article, it would not avoid certain phrases out of a passive aggressive reaction to some editor’s note, it would not ever just rush an article because everyone is gathering at the pub, and it would not choose an analogy just to rub the author of a bitchy critical letter to the editor the wrong way.
sentences.zh: Midjourney 和 Suno 都没有因为短视或腕管而发展出自己的风格。如果你有一个人类作家，在漫长的职业生涯中只根据别人给他们的大纲写文章，而你拥有所有大纲和这些大纲中产生的所有文章，你可以训练法学硕士根据大纲生成一篇文章，它仍然不会因为前一篇文章中的不雅短语而感到沮丧，它不会因为对某些内容的被动攻击性反应而避免某些短语。编者注，它永远不会因为大家都聚集在酒吧而匆匆写一篇文章，它也不会选择一个类比来激怒编辑一封恶毒的批评信的作者。

sentences.en: An LLM could not “subtweet”.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士无法“转推”。

sentences.en: It could not write a series of articles hoping one important person will spot that they are auditioning for a job.Creators have unseen, undocumented influences and motivations that inform their work over a long period.
sentences.zh: 它不可能写一系列文章希望某个重要人物会发现他们正在面试一份工作。创作者有看不见的、没有记录的影响和动机，这些影响和动机在很长一段时间内影响着他们的工作。

sentences.en: I don’t mean to say that these individual influences can be reliably detected in individual pieces of work.
sentences.zh: 我并不是说可以在个别作品中可靠地检测到这些个人影响。

sentences.en: I do mean to say that I think their broad absence tends to be felt in LLM writing.
sentences.zh: 我的意思是说，我认为他们在法学硕士写作中普遍缺席。

sentences.en: As readers we develop an affinity for writers as much as for their writing, and we do this in part because we deduce things about them.
sentences.zh: 作为读者，我们对作家和他们的作品产生了浓厚的兴趣，部分原因是我们推断出有关他们的事情。

blocks.html_en: Very elegantly put! So much so in fact, that I finally put down my phone to grab my laptop in order to write this. I have had a similar much cruder version of this thought: LLMs are not human, they don't know what it's like to stub their toe, despite having read probably millions descriptions of it. Even amongst humans there are experiences which are impossible to share, despite us having evolved to communicate quite effectively. Writing is just a small part of it, and cannot replace the actual experience of being human.

sentences.en: Very elegantly put!
sentences.zh: 非常优雅地放置！

sentences.en: So much so in fact, that I finally put down my phone to grab my laptop in order to write this.
sentences.zh: 事实上，我终于放下手机，拿起笔记本电脑来写这篇文章。

blocks.html_en:
Each person writes in a different personal way, so writing “like a human” would actually require a model being able to purposefully make the specific choices that an individual human writer does.

However, general purpose LLMs like Fable have been trained on huge amounts of all kinds of data, and therefore find it exceedingly hard to break out of the grooves carved by that data. They can’t avoid defaulting to centroids and averages, even when they are trying not to. This makes it possible for classifiers like Pangram to discriminate their writing.

A plausible way to work around this limitation would be to train a LLM on a limited and cohesive subset of writing materials, so it would absorb their specific writing style.

One example might be Talkie, a LLM trained on pre-1930’s English text. Talkie is a far smaller and less powerful model than Fable.

And yet, Talkie’s writing is so distinctive that it is often classified as human by Pangram.

blocks.html_en:
I think it's partly because of where they come from to the problem.

I have education/experience in both literature and coding, I have a pragmatic starting point when approaching text while also being able to recognize stylistic oddities, so I get to be the guy editing out AIsms sometimes.

But I've helped other people copywrite where their environment was all org-speak and academic writing, and AIsms don't really stand out in that case. AI is effectively "doing the right thing" writing the way it does for those tasks. Even tho the right thing is often a bad thing.

sentences.en: I think it's partly because of where they come from to the problem.I have education/experience in both literature and coding, I have a pragmatic starting point when approaching text while also being able to recognize stylistic oddities, so I get to be the guy editing out AIsms sometimes.But I've helped other people copywrite where their environment was all org-speak and academic writing, and AIsms don't really stand out in that case.
sentences.zh: 我认为这部分是因为他们从哪里来解决这个问题。我在文学和编码方面都有教育/经验，在处理文本时我有一个务实的起点，同时也能够识别风格上的奇怪之处，所以我有时会成为编辑人工智能主义的人。但我帮助其他人进行文案写作，他们的环境都是组织语言和学术写作，而人工智能主义在这种情况下并不真正脱颖而出。

sentences.en: AI is effectively "doing the right thing" writing the way it does for those tasks.
sentences.zh: 人工智能正在有效地“做正确的事”，按照它为这些任务所做的方式进行编写。

sentences.en: Even tho the right thing is often a bad thing.
sentences.zh: 即使正确的事情往往是坏事。

blocks.html_en:
AI adopted humanisms, we just weren’t used to seeing them at the same scale we do today.

Diversity of writing styles was part of that, but I’d point to vernacular exposure as the larger component. We’re going to go through a period where we try to adapt to a form of “Universal English” for those of us who read primarily English writing.

Other languages’ readers may be experiencing the same dissonance when they come across AI-generated prose in their native language (but I’ll let others validate /reject my hypothesis).

sentences.en: AI adopted humanisms, we just weren’t used to seeing them at the same scale we do today.Diversity of writing styles was part of that, but I’d point to vernacular exposure as the larger component.
sentences.zh: 人工智能采用了人文主义，我们只是不习惯以今天的规模来看待它们。写作风格的多样性是其中的一部分，但我认为白话的接触是更大的组成部分。

sentences.zh: 我们将经历一个时期，我们试图为我们这些主要阅读英语写作的人适应一种“通用英语”形式。其他语言的读者在遇到人工智能用其母语生成的散文时可能会遇到同样的不和谐（但我会让其他人验证/拒绝我的假设）。

blocks.html_en: It's like people learning a new language according to the book. Those people sound foreign to a native speaker. If AI was trained on school books for grammar, then they too will sound odd to a native speaker even if their output is technically fine or technically more proper. I known nothing of LLM training and how weighting is applied to educational content vs other sources, but it feels like they were weighted away from modern colloquial speak and towards book grammar. The whole thing reminds me of school literature classes where the teachers comments always felt like I was being guided to a more strained sound and less natural. But what do I know. Lit was my least favorite subject which is well evidenced by my grades compared to my math/science scores.

sentences.en: It's like people learning a new language according to the book.
sentences.zh: 这就像人们根据书学习一门新语言一样。

sentences.en: Those people sound foreign to a native speaker.
sentences.zh: 这些人对于母语人士来说听起来很陌生。

sentences.zh: 这些人对于母语人士来说听起来很陌生。

sentences.en: If AI was trained on school books for grammar, then they too will sound odd to a native speaker even if their output is technically fine or technically more proper.
sentences.zh: 如果人工智能接受了语法教科书的训练，那么即使它们的输出在技术上很好或更合适，对于母语人士来说也会听起来很奇怪。

sentences.en: I known nothing of LLM training and how weighting is applied to educational content vs other sources, but it feels like they were weighted away from modern colloquial speak and towards book grammar.
sentences.zh: 我对法学硕士培训以及如何将权重应用于教育内容与其他来源一无所知，但感觉它们的权重远离了现代口语，而转向了书本语法。

sentences.en: The whole thing reminds me of school literature classes where the teachers comments always felt like I was being guided to a more strained sound and less natural.
sentences.zh: 整件事让我想起学校的文学课，老师的评论总是让我感觉自己被引导到一种更紧张、更不自然的声音。

sentences.en: But what do I know.
sentences.zh: 但我知道什么。

sentences.en: Lit was my least favorite subject which is well evidenced by my grades compared to my math/science scores.
sentences.zh: 文学是我最不喜欢的科目，与我的数学/科学分数相比，我的成绩充分证明了这一点。

blocks.html_en:
> we just weren’t used to seeing them at the same scale we do today.

I think there's also a lot of recency bias in it. The same thing that makes you suddenly notice how many people are driving the same model car you just looked at, or how many ads there are for Turbo Encabulators after you read an article about them. A lot of the "tells" people picked out in early AI are tells because they're also really common in the material that the AI was trained on and the styles it was made to emulate. But until everyone wanted "one quick trick" to pick out AI writings, people didn't have any particular reason to need to notice those tells and so they slipped under the radar.

sentences.en: > we just weren’t used to seeing them at the same scale we do today.I think there's also a lot of recency bias in it.
sentences.zh: > 我们只是不习惯以今天的规模看到它们。我认为其中也存在很多新近度偏差。

sentences.en: The same thing that makes you suddenly notice how many people are driving the same model car you just looked at, or how many ads there are for Turbo Encabulators after you read an article about them.
sentences.zh: 同样的事情会让您突然注意到有多少人正在驾驶您刚刚看到的同一型号汽车，或者在您阅读有关 Turbo Encabulators 的文章后有多少广告。

blocks.html_en:
This is right. AI is using human speech, but just doing so in a consistently peculiar way. The em-dash in particular is frustrating, because it's all over high-quality, pre-2022 academic work. But now instead of proper and erudite it's seen as AI-slop. Well, maybe if they didn't use it — all — time — ! It's even an auto-replacement in Word and can be (by one's choice) in LibreOffice, replacing three dashes (and two is replaced by an en-dash).

But when I submit a novel with em-dashes, will sloppy agents and sloppy editors be able to tell that em-dash was deliberately put there by me?

sentences.en: This is right.
sentences.zh: 这是对的。

sentences.en: AI is using human speech, but just doing so in a consistently peculiar way.
sentences.zh: 人工智能正在使用人类语音，但只是以一种一贯独特的方式进行。

sentences.en: The em-dash in particular is frustrating, because it's all over high-quality, pre-2022 academic work.
sentences.zh: 破折号尤其令人沮丧，因为它都是 2022 年之前的高质量学术工作。

sentences.en: But now instead of proper and erudite it's seen as AI-slop.
sentences.zh: 但现在它不再是正确的、博学的，而是被视为人工智能的废话。

sentences.en: Well, maybe if they didn't use it — all — time — !
sentences.zh: 好吧，也许如果他们不使用它——一直——时间——！

sentences.en: It's even an auto-replacement in Word and can be (by one's choice) in LibreOffice, replacing three dashes (and two is replaced by an en-dash).But when I submit a novel with em-dashes, will sloppy agents and sloppy editors be able to tell that em-dash was deliberately put there by me?
sentences.zh: 它甚至是 Word 中的自动替换，并且可以（由个人选择）在 LibreOffice 中替换三个破折号（其中两个被一个破折号替换）。但是，当我提交一本带有破折号的小说时，草率的代理人和草率的编辑是否能够看出破折号是我故意放在那里的？

blocks.html_en:
More noticeable to me is the lack of the work of a good copy editor which, sadly, we haven't had for a really long time. At least, not on the interwebs. Even the news sites reduced where their print copies were known for rigorous editing saw obvious issues with the various corporate overlords doing serious headcount reductions. The rush to be first to publish reduced even further the time any editors might have had, and then the wide spread use of CMS style articles that slammed output together with something as unintelligent as 'cat segmentFromAuthor1 segmentFromAuthor2 segmentFromAuthor3 > article' where you can tell where each segment started over again with the same basic information as if it was content meant to stand on its own.

Of course, the amount of self published work has also helped make the lack of a good copy editor noticeable. I can excuse self published blogs though. But the stuff released "professionally" has really become farcical.

sentences.en: More noticeable to me is the lack of the work of a good copy editor which, sadly, we haven't had for a really long time.
sentences.zh: 对我来说更值得注意的是缺乏优秀的文案编辑的工作，遗憾的是，我们已经很长时间没有这样的工作了。

sentences.en: At least, not on the interwebs.
sentences.zh: 至少，不是在互联网上。

sentences.en: Even the news sites reduced where their print copies were known for rigorous editing saw obvious issues with the various corporate overlords doing serious headcount reductions.
sentences.zh: 即使是那些以严格编辑着称的新闻网站也减少了印刷版的数量，也看到了各种企业霸主严重裁员的明显问题。

sentences.en: The rush to be first to publish reduced even further the time any editors might have had, and then the wide spread use of CMS style articles that slammed output together with something as unintelligent as 'cat segmentFromAuthor1 segmentFromAuthor2 segmentFromAuthor3 > article' where you can tell where each segment started over again with the same basic information as if it was content meant to stand on its own.Of course, the amount of self published work has also helped make the lack of a good copy editor noticeable.
sentences.zh: 急于第一个发布甚至进一步减少了编辑可能拥有的时间，然后广泛使用 CMS 风格的文章，这些文章将输出与诸如“categmentFromAuthor1segmentFromAuthor2segmentFromAuthor3>文章”之类的不智能的东西结合在一起，你可以使用相同的基本信息来判断每个片段从哪里重新开始，就好像它是独立的内容一样。当然，自我发表的作品数量也使得人们注意到缺乏优秀的文案编辑。

sentences.en: I can excuse self published blogs though.
sentences.zh: 不过我可以原谅自己发表的博客。

sentences.en: But the stuff released "professionally" has really become farcical.
sentences.zh: 但“专业”发布的东西却真的变得荒唐可笑。

sentences.zh: 我可以立即嗅出人工智能写作的味道，但据我所知，人工智能写作比以往任何时候都更受欢迎

blocks.html_en:
Very neat site and write-up, but I found this part amusing:

> There is really nothing “AI” about this aside from the tool that collected the data and coded it, and, crucially, semantic search […]

So really, everything about it is AI. And that’s not a bad thing! It’s okay to simultaneously preach the superiority of award winning books over AI-generated garbage while also acknowledging the same AI as a valuable tool for other uses.

sentences.en: Very neat site and write-up, but I found this part amusing:> There is really nothing “AI” about this aside from the tool that collected the data and coded it, and, crucially, semantic search […]So really, everything about it is AI.
sentences.zh: 非常整洁的网站和文章，但我发现这部分很有趣：> 除了收集数据并对其进行编码的工具之外，这实际上没有什么“人工智能”，而且最重要的是，语义搜索 [...]所以真的，关于它的一切都是人工智能。

sentences.en: And that’s not a bad thing!
sentences.zh: 这并不是一件坏事！

sentences.en: It’s okay to simultaneously preach the superiority of award winning books over AI-generated garbage while also acknowledging the same AI as a valuable tool for other uses.
sentences.zh: 可以同时宣传获奖书籍相对于人工智能产生的垃圾的优越性，同时也承认相同的人工智能是用于其他用途的有价值的工具。

blocks.html_en: Key word being tool. People get lost in a fantasy that the AI was responsible for decisions and not the human who chose to use it as a replacement for effort rather than a tool to enhance effort, then apply their bias to all AI use.

sentences.en: Key word being tool.
sentences.zh: 关键词是工具。

sentences.en: People get lost in a fantasy that the AI was responsible for decisions and not the human who chose to use it as a replacement for effort rather than a tool to enhance effort, then apply their bias to all AI use.
sentences.zh: 人们迷失在一种幻想中，认为人工智能负责决策，而不是人类选择使用它作为努力的替代品，而不是增强努力的工具，然后将他们的偏见应用于所有人工智能的使用。

blocks.html_en:
so much of AI is like, to me, used as was to generate basically dynamic applicaions. if we ever got anywhere with semantics, objects, etc, pushed html further, maybe we'd naturally have web clients that could take objects, natively search, collate, display, put on a map, graph, etc... but AI is like a text only shortcut to the end result we want.

I have a stupid AI app I use now for some bookkeeping which was previously, just an excel sheet. except it has a UI, it validates inputs, and does a bunch of other housekeeping stuff that makes it nice. Did the excel work? yes totally.

sentences.en: so much of AI is like, to me, used as was to generate basically dynamic applicaions.
sentences.zh: 对我来说，人工智能的大部分内容就像是用来生成基本上动态的应用程序。

sentences.en: if we ever got anywhere with semantics, objects, etc, pushed html further, maybe we'd naturally have web clients that could take objects, natively search, collate, display, put on a map, graph, etc...
sentences.zh: 如果我们在语义、对象等方面取得了进展，进一步推动了 html，也许我们自然会拥有可以获取对象、本机搜索、整理、显示、放置地图、图表等的 Web 客户端...

sentences.en: but AI is like a text only shortcut to the end result we want.I have a stupid AI app I use now for some bookkeeping which was previously, just an excel sheet.
sentences.zh: 但人工智能就像是我们想要的最终结果的纯文本快捷方式。我有一个愚蠢的人工智能应用程序，我现在用它来做一些簿记，以前只是一张 Excel 表格。

sentences.en: except it has a UI, it validates inputs, and does a bunch of other housekeeping stuff that makes it nice.
sentences.zh: 除了它有一个用户界面之外，它还验证输入，并执行一系列其他使其变得很好的内务处理工作。

sentences.en: Did the excel work?
sentences.zh: excel有效果吗？

sentences.en: yes totally.
sentences.zh: 是的，完全可以。

blocks.html_en:
Fascinated that the author shares an experience of discovery in libraries. The internet, in its early days felt like that. Of course, no longer.

Libraries are certainly declining in their traditional form. I find it odd that everyone has a digital resources in their pocket yet libraries are squeezing out physical books to make way for more and more computers. Try to find paper copy of the Sony founder's book.... that will be £50 on amazon. Prohibitive. No library within a 10 mile radius has a copy.

I remember joyfully discovering Tony Royce's book in my library a few years ago. That enlightenment will never happen now. Primary knowledge is being lost, churned crude will forever lubricate the delusions of those who have no facility to collate the basis of our understanding.

sentences.en: Fascinated that the author shares an experience of discovery in libraries.
sentences.zh: 作者分享了在图书馆发现的经历，这让我很着迷。

sentences.en: The internet, in its early days felt like that.
sentences.zh: 早期的互联网就是这样的感觉。

sentences.en: Of course, no longer.Libraries are certainly declining in their traditional form.
sentences.zh: 当然，不再是了。图书馆的传统形式无疑正在衰落。

sentences.en: I find it odd that everyone has a digital resources in their pocket yet libraries are squeezing out physical books to make way for more and more computers.
sentences.zh: 我觉得奇怪的是，每个人的口袋里都有数字资源，但图书馆却在挤出实体书，为越来越多的计算机让路。

sentences.en: Try to find paper copy of the Sony founder's book....
sentences.zh: 尝试找到索尼创始人的书的纸质副本......

sentences.en: that will be £50 on amazon.
sentences.zh: 亚马逊上的价格是 50 英镑。

sentences.en: Prohibitive.
sentences.zh: 禁止的。

sentences.en: No library within a 10 mile radius has a copy.I remember joyfully discovering Tony Royce's book in my library a few years ago.
sentences.zh: 方圆 10 英里内没有一家图书馆拥有该书。我记得几年前我在我的图书馆高兴地发现了托尼·罗伊斯的书。

sentences.en: That enlightenment will never happen now.
sentences.zh: 这种启蒙现在永远不会发生。

sentences.en: Primary knowledge is being lost, churned crude will forever lubricate the delusions of those who have no facility to collate the basis of our understanding.
sentences.zh: 原始知识正在消失，粗制滥造的知识将永远润滑那些无法整理我们的理解基​​础的人的妄想。

blocks.html_en:
> The internet, in its early days felt like that. Of course, no longer.

back in the reasonably early days of the web (I would guess ~2000) I stumbled across a webpage that said "I used to collect random interesting snippets in a shoebox, here is the internet version of it". I was delighted enough that I actually wrote to the author to compliment them on helping keep the web interesting, and though I haven't thought about it in ages it clearly made enough of an impression on me that I remembered the author's name. and it's still up! https://www-users.york.ac.uk/~ss44/cyc/index.htm

sentences.en: > The internet, in its early days felt like that.
sentences.zh: > 早期的互联网就是这样的感觉。

sentences.en: Of course, no longer.back in the reasonably early days of the web (I would guess ~2000) I stumbled across a webpage that said "I used to collect random interesting snippets in a shoebox, here is the internet version of it".
sentences.zh: 当然，不再是了。早在网络的早期（我猜大约是 2000 年），我偶然发现了一个网页，上面写着“我曾经在鞋盒中收集随机有趣的片段，这是它的互联网版本”。

sentences.en: I was delighted enough that I actually wrote to the author to compliment them on helping keep the web interesting, and though I haven't thought about it in ages it clearly made enough of an impression on me that I remembered the author's name.
sentences.zh: 我很高兴我实际上写信给作者，赞扬他们帮助保持网络的有趣，虽然我已经很久没有考虑过这一点，但它显然给我留下了足够的印象，我记住了作者的名字。

sentences.en: and it's still up!
sentences.zh: 而且还没完！

sentences.en: https://www-users.york.ac.uk/~ss44/cyc/index.htm

sentences.zh: 我很高兴我实际上写信给作者，赞扬他们帮助保持网络的有趣，虽然我已经很久没有考虑过这一点，但它显然给我留下了足够的印象，我记住了作者的名字。

sentences.en: and it's still up!
sentences.zh: 而且还没完！

sentences.en: https://www-users.york.ac.uk/~ss44/cyc/index.htm

blocks.html_en:
AIs are fantastic for discovery. Just today I asked Chatgpt to find blogs 1) about old school newspaper cartoons 2) that have been writing for more than 10 years and 3) are run by one or two passionate people rather than a team. Within seconds Chatgpt found 7 candidates. Within another handful of seconds it gave me RSS links to the ones I wanted.

This is incredible!

sentences.en: AIs are fantastic for discovery.
sentences.zh: 人工智能非常适合发现。

sentences.en: Just today I asked Chatgpt to find blogs 1) about old school newspaper cartoons 2) that have been writing for more than 10 years and 3) are run by one or two passionate people rather than a team.
sentences.zh: 就在今天，我要求 Chatgpt 寻找以下博客：1) 关于旧学校报纸漫画的博客；2) 已经写作了 10 多年的博客；3) 由一两个充满热情的人而不是一个团队运营的博客。

sentences.en: Within seconds Chatgpt found 7 candidates.
sentences.zh: Chatgpt 在几秒钟内找到了 7 个候选者。

sentences.en: Within another handful of seconds it gave me RSS links to the ones I wanted.This is incredible!
sentences.zh: 又过了几秒钟，它就给了我我想要的 RSS 链接。这太不可思议了！

blocks.html_en:
I like very very much that there's no user voting system here.

I just recently read a really mediocre book with a 4.7/5 rating on Goodreads. Shows being sloppy is not exclusively a privilege for neither the AIs or producers.

sentences.en: I like very very much that there's no user voting system here.I just recently read a really mediocre book with a 4.7/5 rating on Goodreads.
sentences.zh: 我非常喜欢这里没有用户投票系统。我最近刚刚读了一本非常平庸的书，在 Goodreads 上的评分为 4.7/5。

sentences.en: Shows being sloppy is not exclusively a privilege for neither the AIs or producers.
sentences.zh: 节目马虎并不完全是人工智能或制片人的特权。

blocks.html_en:
This is pretty nice, I might use this if I'm looking to learn more on a topic.

I'm always cautious when reading nonfiction because it's hard for me to tell if the author knows what they're talking about when I'm not an expert myself. Using awards is a good metric. Maybe.

I am curious what the "score" for each book means. Is it calculated by giving each award a certain weight and adding them up?

sentences.zh: 也许吧。我很好奇每本书的“分数”是什么意思。

sentences.en: Is it calculated by giving each award a certain weight and adding them up?
sentences.zh: 是给每个奖项赋予一定的权重，然后相加来计算的吗？

blocks.html_en: I'm not sure how I feel about "Entangled Life" on the first page of books in "science". Isn't that slop? I mean, it's pop-science bullshit, and it's based on feelgood extrapolation from a thin evidence base. Maybe that doesn't quite fit the definition of slop, but it's not a signal of quality for the rest of the list.

sentences.en: I'm not sure how I feel about "Entangled Life" on the first page of books in "science".
sentences.zh: 我不知道我对《科学》书籍第一页上的《纠缠的生命》有何感想。

sentences.en: Isn't that slop?
sentences.zh: 这不是烂尾吗？

sentences.en: I mean, it's pop-science bullshit, and it's based on feelgood extrapolation from a thin evidence base.
sentences.zh: 我的意思是，这是通俗科学的废话，它是基于从薄弱的证据基础上做出的感觉良好的推断。

sentences.en: Maybe that doesn't quite fit the definition of slop, but it's not a signal of quality for the rest of the list.
sentences.zh: 也许这不太符合溢出的定义，但这并不是列表中其余部分的质量信号。

blocks.html_en:
The antithesis of AI slop is when you've created a unique idea from first principles and AI keeps misunderstanding your idea and spell checking it back to something that's widely popular such that you have to write a whole extra alignment subsystem to check that it hasn't ignored half of what you've wrote in your first principles document and just thought you were talking about something distantly related in its training set.

To work on something truly original in non-fiction in the future against AI pushing everything to the mean of its training set and fighting against that when doing research, as opposed to generating a whole book of slop from familiar ideas, is going to be quite the unrecognized effort of writing those books.

sentences.en: The antithesis of AI slop is when you've created a unique idea from first principles and AI keeps misunderstanding your idea and spell checking it back to something that's widely popular such that you have to write a whole extra alignment subsystem to check that it hasn't ignored half of what you've wrote in your first principles document and just thought you were talking about something distantly related in its training set.To work on something truly original in non-fiction in the future against AI pushing everything to the mean of its training set and fighting against that when doing research, as opposed to generating a whole book of slop from familiar ideas, is going to be quite the unrecognized effort of writing those books.
sentences.zh: AI slop 的对立面是，当你从第一原理中创建了一个独特的想法，而 AI 不断地误解你的想法，并将其拼写检查回广泛流行的东西，这样你就必须编写一个完整的额外对齐子系统来检查它是否忽略了你在第一原理文档中所写的一半内容，并且只是认为你正在谈论其训练集中遥远相关的东西。为了在未来致力于非小说类中真正原创的东西，反对 AI 将一切都推向其训练集的平均值，并在做研究，而不是从熟悉的想法中写出一整本书，这将是写这些书的不被认可的努力。

To work on something truly original in non-fiction in the future against AI pushing everything to the mean of its training set and fighting against that when doing research, as opposed to generating a whole book of slop from familiar ideas, is going to be quite the unrecognized effort of writing those books.

blocks.html_en:
I participate book club for several years with friends, we casually navigate certain topics, and i can tell you human slop in literature is a real thing.

- almost every book try to stretch core idea into book size format

- unique ideas are rare people attack them under different angels

- a later phenomena : their believes almost predict entire book, outcomes etc, brainwash impact is real

so not sure, how ai slop is better vs book slop, at least with ai you can distill the idea, with the book, you have to spend 10-40 hours to digest average, absolutely non fresh ideas, that author brought in just to sell that book, otherwise it would be magazine article worth.

sentences.en: I participate book club for several years with friends, we casually navigate certain topics, and i can tell you human slop in literature is a real thing.- almost every book try to stretch core idea into book size format- unique ideas are rare people attack them under different angels- a later phenomena : their believes almost predict entire book, outcomes etc, brainwash impact is realso not sure, how ai slop is better vs book slop, at least with ai you can distill the idea, with the book, you have to spend 10-40 hours to digest average, absolutely non fresh ideas, that author brought in just to sell that book, otherwise it would be magazine article worth.
sentences.zh: 我和朋友一起参加了好几年的读书俱乐部，我们随意浏览某些主题，我可以告诉你文学中的人性溢出是真实存在的。-几乎每本书都试图将核心思想延伸到书籍大小的格式中-独特的想法很少有人从不同的角度攻击它们-后来的现象：他们的信念几乎预测整本书，结果等，洗脑影响是真实的所以不确定，人工智能的溢出比书本的溢出更好，至少用人工智能你可以提炼出想法，有了书，你必须花时间10-40小时来消化平均的、绝对不新鲜的想法，作者只是为了卖那本书而引入的，否则它就值杂志文章了。

blocks.html_en:
> how ai slop is better vs book slop, at least with ai you can distill the idea

Why don't you just use whatever LLM you have on hand to summarize every book you could read instead of reading it? The idea that ai slop is 'better' because you can distill the idea is an insane point to me because it treats books as a pure consumption-based concept, where the goal is strictly to finish a book and move on.

I think even the worst book is more valuable than whatever garbage an LLM writes because you can at least learn from the intent. Where and why the author failed, where they got stuck, the points they failed to make. LLM writing is a void, there's nothing to be learned.

sentences.en: > how ai slop is better vs book slop, at least with ai you can distill the ideaWhy don't you just use whatever LLM you have on hand to summarize every book you could read instead of reading it?
sentences.zh: > ai slop 与 book slop 相比如何更好，至少有了 ai 你可以提炼出这个想法你为什么不直接使用你手头上的任何 LLM 来总结你可以读的每一本书而不是阅读它呢？

sentences.en: The idea that ai slop is 'better' because you can distill the idea is an insane point to me because it treats books as a pure consumption-based concept, where the goal is strictly to finish a book and move on.I think even the worst book is more valuable than whatever garbage an LLM writes because you can at least learn from the intent.

sentences.zh: LLM写作是空的，没什么可学的。

blocks.html_en:
> how ai slop is better vs book slop, at least with ai you can distill the idea

Why don't you just use whatever LLM you have on hand to summarize every book you could read instead of reading it? The idea that ai slop is 'better' because you can distill the idea is an insane point to me because it treats books as a pure consumption-based concept, where the goal is strictly to finish a book and move on.

I think even the worst book is more valuable than whatever garbage an LLM writes because you can at least learn from the intent. Where and why the author failed, where they got stuck, the points they failed to make. LLM writing is a void, there's nothing to be learned.

I understand your passion is non-fiction, but would be wonderful to have the equivalent for fiction, of which there is a great deal of excellent literature. Perhaps the same site could incorporate both fiction and non-fiction, with a filter?

blocks.html_en:
I don't think we would have AI slop, if they were just trained on high quality media.

Less relatable AI, maybe, but not sloppy AI.

AI trained on other AI (i.e. distilling), can be better than the AI they were trained on, likely because of this reason. Each iteration can raise the bar.

As apposed to the deep spiral of generating shiny attention crystals for humans, and then training on that.

sentences.en: I don't think we would have AI slop, if they were just trained on high quality media.Less relatable AI, maybe, but not sloppy AI.AI trained on other AI (i.e. distilling), can be better than the AI they were trained on, likely because of this reason.
sentences.zh: 我认为，如果他们只是在高质量媒体上接受训练，我们就不会有人工智能的马虎。也许，不太相关的人工智能，但不会马虎的人工智能。在其他人工智能（即蒸馏）上训练的人工智能可能比他们接受训练的人工智能更好，可能是因为这个原因。

sentences.en: Each iteration can raise the bar.As apposed to the deep spiral of generating shiny attention crystals for humans, and then training on that.
sentences.zh: 每次迭代都可以提高标准。与为人类生成闪亮的注意力晶体，然后对其进行训练的深层螺旋相反。

blocks.html_en: The slop isn't a property of the LLM but the workflow it's utilized in. Just now I asked ChatGPT about the linguistic effect where a CRT could be described as a giant flatscreen, a landline could be described as a wired cellphone, or the original Rogue could be described as a roguelike. Unfortunately it doesn't know what that's called either, but that is a non-slop use of an LLM, like a semantic search engine. On the other hand if you print LLM output into a book it's almost certainly slop no matter how the model was trained.

sentences.en: The slop isn't a property of the LLM but the workflow it's utilized in.
sentences.zh: slop不是LLM的属性，而是它所使用的工作流程的属性。

sentences.en: Just now I asked ChatGPT about the linguistic effect where a CRT could be described as a giant flatscreen, a landline could be described as a wired cellphone, or the original Rogue could be described as a roguelike.
sentences.zh: 刚才我向 ChatGPT 询问了语言效果，CRT 可以被描述为一个巨大的平板屏幕，固定电话可以被描述为有线手机，或者最初的 Rogue 可以被描述为 roguelike。

sentences.en: Unfortunately it doesn't know what that's called either, but that is a non-slop use of an LLM, like a semantic search engine.
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，它也不知道这叫什么，但这是对法学硕士的不遗余力的使用，就像语义搜索引擎一样。

sentences.en: On the other hand if you print LLM output into a book it's almost certainly slop no matter how the model was trained.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，如果将 LLM 输出打印到书中，无论模型如何训练，几乎肯定会出现问题。

blocks.html_en: Training AI on AI will most likely increase slop. LLM's add randomness in their outputs, books usually don't.

sentences.en: Training AI on AI will most likely increase slop.
sentences.zh: 在人工智能上训练人工智能很可能会增加斜率。

sentences.en: LLM's add randomness in their outputs, books usually don't.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士在其输出中增加了随机性，而书籍通常不会。

blocks.html_en:
My bona-fides here amount to little more than being a big time reader of non-fiction. But IMO, there's a nice synthesis here. Rather than going long rounds of asking LLMs about a subject, I usually end up asking it for book recommendations. It's much better than a google search and you can push it into some deep corners if you go past the surface level recs.

You can get quite specific. You can find texts you wouldn't discover unless you spent years studying the topic. Often these are completely approachable and give interesting perspectives they just get buried behind a wall of syllabi and listicles.

This is how I ended up reading Thompson's 'The Making of the English Working Class' and Graves' 'Goodbye to all That' among others.

sentences.en: My bona-fides here amount to little more than being a big time reader of non-fiction.
sentences.zh: 我的真诚只不过是非小说类书籍的忠实读者。

sentences.en: But IMO, there's a nice synthesis here.
sentences.zh: 但在我看来，这里有一个很好的综合。

sentences.en: Rather than going long rounds of asking LLMs about a subject, I usually end up asking it for book recommendations.
sentences.zh: 我通常不会向法学硕士询问某个主题的问题，而是向他们询问书籍推荐。

sentences.en: It's much better than a google search and you can push it into some deep corners if you go past the surface level recs.You can get quite specific.
sentences.zh: 它比谷歌搜索要好得多，如果您超越了表面水平的建议，您可以将其推入某些深处。您可以获得非常具体的信息。

sentences.en: You can find texts you wouldn't discover unless you spent years studying the topic.
sentences.zh: 您可以找到除非您花费数年时间研究该主题，否则您不会发现的文本。

sentences.en: Often these are completely approachable and give interesting perspectives they just get buried behind a wall of syllabi and listicles.This is how I ended up reading Thompson's 'The Making of the English Working Class' and Graves' 'Goodbye to all That' among others.
sentences.zh: 通常这些都是完全平易近人的，并给出了有趣的观点，但它们只是被埋在大纲和清单的墙后面。这就是我最终阅读汤普森的《英国工人阶级的形成》和格雷夫斯的《再见这一切》等书的原因。

sentences.en: My bona-fides here amount to little more than being a big time reader of non-fiction.
sentences.zh: 我的真诚只不过是非小说类书籍的忠实读者。

sentences.en: But IMO, there's a nice synthesis here.
sentences.zh: 但在我看来，这里有一个很好的综合。

sentences.en: Rather than going long rounds of asking LLMs about a subject, I usually end up asking it for book recommendations.
sentences.zh: 我通常不会向法学硕士询问某个主题的问题，而是向他们询问书籍推荐。

sentences.en: It's much better than a google search and you can push it into some deep corners if you go past the surface level recs.
sentences.zh: 它比谷歌搜索要好得多，如果你超越了表面水平的搜索，你可以将它推入一些深处的角落。

blocks.html_en: You can get quite specific. You can find texts you wouldn't discover unless you spent years studying the topic. Often these are completely approachable and give interesting perspectives they just get buried behind a wall of syllabi and listicles.

sentences.zh: 您可以找到除非您花费数年时间研究该主题，否则您不会发现的文本。

sentences.en: Often these are completely approachable and give interesting perspectives they just get buried behind a wall of syllabi and listicles.
sentences.zh: 通常这些都是完全平易近人的，并且提供了有趣的观点，但它们只是被埋在教学大纲和清单的墙后面。

blocks.html_en: I read at night in bed. I love how, with ebooks (first Overdrive, now Libby), when I hear about an interesting book, I can within 30 seconds search for it across multiple libraries, borrow it, and send it to my Kindle.

blocks.html_en:
And what pray tell is everyone doing with their privileged leisure time?

You have access to every single wicked problem in the world and you choose to do nothing.

Shame

blocks.html_en: Shame

It's sad now to see a university library where students sit among endless shelves of amazing books, sitting on laptops, almost all of them with ChatGPT open. The library has become just a place to sit and open an LLM.

sentences.en: It's sad now to see a university library where students sit among endless shelves of amazing books, sitting on laptops, almost all of them with ChatGPT open.
sentences.zh: 现在看到大学图书馆的学生们坐在无尽的书架上，书架上摆满了精彩的书籍，坐在笔记本电脑上，几乎所有的笔记本电脑都打开了 ChatGPT，真是令人难过。

sentences.en: The library has become just a place to sit and open an LLM.
sentences.zh: 图书馆已经变成了一个坐下来开办法学硕士学位的地方。

blocks.html_en: The most sad part of all is to think how little of that knowledge is digitized and available to be referenced by ChatGPT. Digitizing old books is one of the highest things you can do as a human for “good for the world”.

blocks.html_en: It's especially sad because on of the things those LLMs are best, almost purpose built for is to tell those students which books to go open, where to find nuggets people haven't bumped into for years, to make cross-connections that would take a PhD a decade to find.

blocks.html_en:
I think my issue with this project--and so many other similar ones--is that the provenance of the code does undermine the intention. If a project purports to be about quality, then knowing that the creator abdicated some of the responsibility for creating the thing they ostensibly care about makes it harder to put faith in them as having high standards elsewhere.

Perhaps I am just old-fashioned, and vibe-coding is something that can be done with full focus and care for high quality, but I remain unconvinced. This is a project that needs to be cared about sincerely to be trustable/meaningful/useful, and the approach taken casts doubt on that.

sentences.en: I think my issue with this project--and so many other similar ones--is that the provenance of the code does undermine the intention.
sentences.zh: 我认为我对这个项目以及许多其他类似项目的问题是代码的出处确实破坏了意图。

sentences.en: If a project purports to be about quality, then knowing that the creator abdicated some of the responsibility for creating the thing they ostensibly care about makes it harder to put faith in them as having high standards elsewhere.Perhaps I am just old-fashioned, and vibe-coding is something that can be done with full focus and care for high quality, but I remain unconvinced.
sentences.zh: 如果一个项目声称是关于质量的，那么知道创建者放弃了创造他们表面上关心的东西的一些责任，就会让人更难相信他们在其他地方有高标准。也许我只是守旧，振动编码是可以全神贯注并关心高质量的事情，但我仍然不相信。

sentences.en: This is a project that needs to be cared about sincerely to be trustable/meaningful/useful, and the approach taken casts doubt on that.
sentences.zh: 这是一个需要真诚关心才能值得信赖/有意义/有用的项目，而所采取的方法却让人对此产生怀疑。

blocks.html_en:
I think my issue with this project--and so many other similar ones--is that the provenance of the code does undermine the intention. If a project purports to be about quality, then knowing that the creator abdicated some of the responsibility for creating the thing they ostensibly care about makes it harder to put faith in them as having high standards elsewhere.

Perhaps I am just old-fashioned, and vibe-coding is something that can be done with full focus and care for high quality, but I remain unconvinced. This is a project that needs to be cared about sincerely to be trustable/meaningful/useful, and the approach taken casts doubt on that.

sentences.en: I think my issue with this project--and so many other similar ones--is that the provenance of the code does undermine the intention.
sentences.zh: 我认为我对这个项目以及许多其他类似项目的问题是代码的出处确实破坏了意图。

sentences.zh: 如果一个项目声称是关于质量的，那么知道创建者放弃了创造他们表面上关心的东西的一些责任，就会让人更难相信他们在其他地方有高标准。也许我只是守旧，振动编码是可以全神贯注并关心高质量的事情，但我仍然不相信。

content_html_zh: 并不是故意逆反，但我对此思考了很多：我认为从根本上来说，好的小说实际上是人工智能的对立面。 法学硕士实际上只能将不同的东西组合成输出。 这可以给你一些创造性的结果，但它最终是有限的。 我无法想象提示人工智能一千次会给你带来像许多高质量虚构故事那样真正原创的东西。 生活的魔幻与荒诞正是创作此类作品所必需的东西。> LLM确实只能将不同的东西组合成输出> 生活的神奇和荒诞是创作这样的作品所必需的。这两点似乎有点矛盾。 为什么如果人类确实比人工智能更具创造力，如果人类不需要“将事物组合成输出”就能发挥创造力，那么它必须体验“生命的神奇和荒谬”吗？ 难道一个有创造力的人不会将他们的经验拼接并重新组合成他们的创造性产出吗？ 因此，如果人工智能拥有更多以某种方式反映这种体验的数据，它是否具有足够的创造力来创造“真正原创”的东西？我的观点是，智力和创造力都只是纯粹的重组。 人工智能可以像人类一样做得很好。 差距在于，与人工智能目前训练的数据相比，人类可以获得的数据更加模拟、更加情绪化、更加扎根于现实世界。> 因此，如果人工智能拥有更多以某种方式反映该经验的数据，它是否具有足够的创造力来创造“真正原创”的东西？一个重要的区别似乎在于人类拥有原始的经验可供借鉴，而人工智能只有“反映”经验的数据。ChatGPT 的对话比你我都多。 它是在更广泛的情况下与来自更广泛背景的人进行对话。我们在学校都是这么做的，不是吗？没有人做任何原创性的研究，你没有发现重力，你没有参与任何具有历史意义的事件。但我们说受过教育的人都知道这些事情。这就是描述性知识。 不是第一手的感受。您可能从有关该主题的每一本书中了解美国内战的每一个细节。 你可以阅读每个写过有关战争的人的每一篇日记。你对参加战争的感受没有第一手的了解。 任何对这种知识的假设都是对你认为一个人基于人格、心理学等当代观念“会想到或感受到”的（错误）概括。阅读一篇关于一名士兵看到他的战友被杀的日记并不等于你自己真正经历过这种经历。他可能会根据其他人的经验告诉你一些相关信息。 并不意味着他真的知道自己在说什么。他可以告诉你一系列的描述。 不是第一手的实际知识。阅读无数的烹饪书是否意味着您知道每种口味的成分是什么？这是一种非常缺乏的知识理论。 如果你从来没有尝过任何东西，你怎么知道什么是品味？如果你读了一千本关于纽约市生活的书，这与亲自在那里生活所获得的经验知识是一回事吗？ 我当然不这么认为。> 而人工智能只有“反映”体验的数据。对于当前的许多人工智能来说都是如此。 有了更多的机器人身体，更多地访问相机/音频/卫星/科学测量，人工智能可以拥有与人类相比只能被描述为神一般水平的原始体验。你可能会说人工智能已经部分地访问了这些测量结果。 您可以轻松地利用智能传感器数据等、相同的视频/音频等。人工智能（尚）无法真正复制的部分是“感觉/情感”。 经历这样的感觉恐惧、孤独、幸福。 或者感到焦虑、紧张或起鸡皮疙瘩。 您可以描述所有这些状态，但每个人的体验都不同，并且还取决于每个独特的情况。当然，这也取决于您如何准确定义情绪。在这一点上，你已经脱离了科幻小说，超出了对此进行合理讨论的范围。即便如此，我也不清楚一个与人完全相同的外观的机器人是否会拥有相同的体验。 它们不是经过数百万年进化的生物。我认为你只是忽略了一个事实，即法学硕士只存在于虚拟世界中，而我们正在与一个更丰富、有更多东西可以提供的世界互动。 虚拟世界仍然只是我们世界的一个子集，但我们是否想要这样感知它取决于每个人。 当然感觉就像你将数字与模拟混为一谈，而数字信号仍然只能近似模拟信号。我仍然怀疑我们是否有知识将我们所有的人类欲望描述为我们的大脑与神经系统相互作用的结果。 除了分析和综合之外，还有许多过程正在进行。 如果我打算做一些非凡的事情，我不会只是坐在那里思考，并尝试根据记忆重新排列一些片段。 我会环顾四周、触摸、品尝、重新探索和扩展我的环境所提供的感知。 此外，我的感知可能每时每刻都在变化，我什至可以在任何愿意的时候尝试引入一点机会。维特根斯坦的“一个人不能说话的地方，一个人必须保持沉默”在这里可能很合适，但语言仍然在不断发展。模型越来越多地显示出它们推断人类未经探索的能力（数学证明是最明显的）。 是什么让你相信生活的荒谬性对于模特来说是独一无二的？数学证明很难获得，但验证起来很容易（或“便宜”）。 此外，通过变压器处理数学证明以得出与现有证明模式相匹配的输出是有意义的。 前者允许您生成数百或数千次迭代，并以低廉的成本验证其正确性，直到达到正确的随机性水平。 生成数千本小说，然后验证它们是否真的有效。大多数优秀的小说作者都在尝试传达他们脑海中正在形成的故事。 即使他们陷入困境，他们通常也不会陷入困境，因为他们只是不知道可以询问的人。 这将不再是他们的声音。 有些人确实寻求意见，有些人可能会尝试通过 llms 寻求想法。 使用 llms 进行研究、技术问题、语法、同义词句子、单词选择等也是有意义的。我同意人们通过 llms 生成完整故事的 OP。 当我这么说时，我认识一个非常年轻的人，他不久前曾说过（比如早期的 ChatGPT 4 时间）他们用它来为他们喜欢的书籍或角色写同人小说。能够写下一系列逻辑上正确的句子与凭直觉判断人类的情感体验并不是一回事。 甚至相关。对我来说，数学似乎与人类的经历完全无关，至少从“这件事发生在我身上，我会把它变成一件艺术品”的意义上来说是这样。 数学是纯粹的抽象。> 法学硕士实际上只能将不同的东西组合成输出就作家如何看待创造力而言，这并不能反映现实——老作家说只有7个故事（以及这个想法的其他变体），而写作就是创造性地重新混合这些陈旧的想法。法学硕士写出可接受的小说和非小说的能力很快就会出现，如果它还没有出现的话。 （我认为现在它仍然需要人类的一些高水平的输入，具体取决于长度和主题）我很清楚，特别是随着法学硕士变得越来越好，只有一个真正的区别——那就是我不想听法学硕士必须说的话。 我只对其他人说的话感兴趣。 我对人工智能音乐也有同样的感觉——即使它听起来不错，甚至和那种不是人工智能制作的非原创流行音乐（而是一种它自己的废话）或一部糟糕的委员会制作的好莱坞电影一样好，它从根本上仍然比生成的东西更有趣。 即使它100%是虚构的，它仍然是基于真实人类的生活经历。这有点像说“只有五种基本口味”，一切都是它们的某种组合。也许吧，但这对实际吃食物的体验基本上为零。 仅仅因为形式可能相同并不意味着法学硕士能够提供原创内容。我不确定我是否理解这个类比。 您可以说，人工香料之所以在食品中发挥作用，是因为我们知道如何从第一原理化学中重新创造风味。 对人造奇多的厌恶也并不能阻止很多人吃它们。我想说，人类制作的草稿写作并不比法学硕士写作的质量更好或更差。知道它是否是由真人制作的对我来说很重要，但我也知道这对每个人来说都不会。仅仅因为一个故事的形式与另一个故事相同，并不意味着内容可以互换。我现在可以给你写一个关于 2026 年住在波兰的外籍人士的故事。 我不清楚法学硕士如何拥有文化知识或经验来写这样一个实际上准确且最新的故事，而数据集中却没有像我的故事这样的东西。你更喜欢看哪一个：超级英雄战争迷航 42 - 最新的系列电影，数十亿美元的好莱坞预算，由亿万富翁资助和制作，经过观众测试的委员会制作的剧本，很多人参与，主要旨在推销商品。个人故事 - 一部关于某人的个人现实或幻想的电影，通常不是由工作室制作的，因为这个故事对广大观众来说没有利润，由一个人使用人工智能精心生成和编辑，主要设计用于讲述一个故事。我提出了另一个稻草人因为你的稻草人太难打了。 作为人类，我们当然都对其他人所说的话感兴趣。 但你还没有解释为什么你觉得人们不能用人工智能来说话，或者为什么人工智能输出不能基于真实人类的生活经历。\就我个人而言，电影制片厂似乎只有7个故事可讲，我看过这部电影及其续集、前传、后传、翻拍、坚韧重启、音乐剧和动画系列。 我很高兴人工智能将让人们跳过工作室的看门人来讲述一些新的故事以进行改变。那么现在我们要研究一下什么是“原创”？以我看来，AI绝对能够创造出新的作品。 数据集中不存在但看起来可能存在的事物。 这几乎就是泛化的意思，他们可以创造出各种各样的东西，这些东西实际上不在原始集合中，但可以很好地隐藏在那个集合中。当你是一个原始文化时，你的意思是什么？人工智能是否可以写一个关于 2026 年作为一名艺术家在纽约生活、文化环境等的故事，而无需在发生时立即将这些信息添加到数据集中？对我来说，很明显，有些文化趋势是瞬时发生的，而不仅仅是过去历史数据的某种结果。理想情况下，人工智能不会从模型权重中获得任何世界知识，而是完全依赖于上下文。 但这只是一个理想，我们还没有弄清楚如何将世界知识与模型完全分离。法学硕士实际上只能将不同的事物组合成输出，是什么让人们相信人类可以做的比这更多？这是一个真正的问题。 我见过很多人说人类的思想“不止于此”，好像这是显而易见的我想说，人脑固有的混乱和连续性可以以一种与法学硕士完全不同的方式创造新奇事物。就像人类可以深入到自己情感的混乱中，并从中提取出独特的东西一样。 在人类经验的某些层面上，想法可以根据你所有的记忆发酵和改变，这是很奇怪的方式，而法学硕士完全缺乏这一点，这使得法学硕士创造的东西非常生硬和明显。 就像他们所做的任何事情都没有细微差别一样。既然人类文化已经从非常有限的石器时代文化发展成为我们今天所拥有的文化，我们必须得出结论，至少有些人有能力创造出真正新的东西。图书价格指数的历史部分非常偏向于美国。 美国历史、美洲原住民、二战后的日本以及上世纪的越南。这就是销售的内容。 美国有很多人，所以即使在像历史这样的小众学科中，卖给我们也会赚钱。那些不想向大众市场销售书籍的历史学家拥有更广泛的书籍。 在普通的书店里你不会看到它们。我敢打赌，你的大脑存储从长文本中读取的信息的方式与存储从与法学硕士聊天中获得的信息的方式非常不同。 当我读到一些对我来说具有挑战性或新鲜的东西时，我会花很多时间思考我所读的内容如何与我自己的经验或知识相匹配。 虽然我的阅读速度相当快，但我经常要花很长时间才能读完困难的页面，因为我必须停下来思考我正在阅读的内容。 我似乎正在做很多整合和重组我的想法。 当与法学硕士互动时，感觉更像是我只是被动地接收知识，而且我认为它也没有得到整合。 不知道为什么会这样，而且有点违反直觉，因为我认为我不会与人类导师有同样的经历。你所描述的与教育理论所说的“同化”和“适应”非常接近。 当我们吸收知识时，它恰好符合我们的理解模式。 当我们容纳知识时，我们需要改变我们的图式，这就是受到挑战的感觉（通常是深刻的感觉）的来源。例如，一个孩子了解到“外国人”意味着来自国外的人。 然后，当他们 11 岁时，他们第一次出国度假，并意识到“等等！_我_在这里是外国人！”因此，也许可以解释您所说的话的一种方式是，LLM 输出往往很容易被同化。我在散步和徒步旅行时听有声读物，有时当我回忆起我学到的一些特定的东西时，我也能回忆起当我听到它时我正在徒步旅行的地方。我认为关于我们如何真正处理信息，还有很多东西需要学习。我曾经听过一场关于“学习如何学习”的讲座。 关于我们处理信息的方式，已经有很多经过深入研究的东西可以利用。 你所描述的被称为检索线索。 我们的思维似乎具有联想能力^1。 似乎很多学习/处理都是在实际学习过程之后发生的，这就是为什么休息很重要。 间隔重复是一种在实践中得到充分证明的进一步方法。^1 在人工智能的早期，赫布学习的概念很流行（“神经元一起放电，连接在一起”）。 它的实现甚至比今天使用的梯度下降更简单，但从未在 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hebbian_theory 上流行起来这就是空间记忆！ 我和朋友们散步聊天时间很长，有时我会忘记之前讨论的一些小细节，但不记得我们提到这件事时我们所处的位置。我的很多朋友都做不到同样的技巧。 但令人着迷的是，它仍然对他们有效！ 通常，在我描述了我们所在的地方后，他们可以回忆起我忘记的任何事情。同样在这里。 我总是对此感到惊奇。 这些回忆甚至可以是多年后的事，而我听到所记得的事情的地点却历历在目。我在某个电脑游戏的加载部分读了一本学校布置的书。 这两个故事在我的脑海里是完全相连的。我十几岁时就听过一个播客。 十多年后，我现在正在重新聆听它，我清楚地记得我从公共汽车站步行回家时，我的 iPod 中通过有线耳机播放了它。> 与法学硕士互动时，感觉更像是我只是被动地接受知识...... 这几乎是一个矛盾，与我的经历非常不同。在大学里，我在学习线性代数时经常使用mathematica。 我后来不得不重新学习很多数学知识，因为我从来没有真正深入地理解它，尽管我能够在课堂条件下使用和应用它。 我觉得我的很多 LLM 衍生知识同样很肤浅。这是绝对正确的。 数学如果不写在纸上就很难理解。 正如我的一位教授所说，“当你从头开始写下一个问题并解决它时，神奇的事情就会发生”。
